chunks.summary: This chapter provides a comprehensive overview of the Equivalent Concept in Chemistry, covering definitions of equivalent weight, n-factor (valency factor) calculation for elements, acids, bases, and salts, and the concept of Normality. It extensively details various types of titrations, including acid-base, redox (Permanganate), and Iodometric/Iodimetric methods, emphasizing the Law of Equivalence. The chapter also addresses advanced topics like the drawbacks of the equivalent concept, volume strength of hydrogen peroxide, water hardness measurement, and oleum strength calculation, supported by numerous solved examples.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: Miscellaneous Problems: Iodometric Titration Molarity (Q12)
Content: 12. An aqueous solution containing 0.1 g KIO₃ (formula weight = 214) and an excess of KI was acidified with HCl. The liberated I₂ consumed 45 mL of thiosulphate. The molarity of sodium thiosulphate solution is :
The reaction involved is :  $ IO_{3}^{-} + I^{-} + H^{+} \longrightarrow I_{2} + H_{2}O $
(A) 0.0623 M (B) 0.0313 M (C) 0.126 M (D) 0.252 M
Sol.  $ IO_{3}^{-} + 5I^{-} + H^{+} \longrightarrow 3I_{2} + H_{2}O $
$ 2Na_{2}S_{2}O_{3} + I_{2} \longrightarrow 2NaI + Na_{2}S_{4}O_{6} $
Now, Moles of KIO₃ =  $ \frac{0.1}{214} $
So, Moles of I₂ = 3 ×  $ \frac{0.1}{214} $
Now, Moles of Na₂S₂O₃ = 2 × 3 ×  $ \frac{0.1}{214} $
∴  $ M \times V_{L} = 2 \times 3 \times \frac{0.1}{214} $  ∴  $ M \times \frac{45}{1000} = 2 \times 3 \times \frac{0.1}{214} $
Now, Molarity of hypo solution =  $ 2 \times 3 \times \frac{0.1}{214} \times \frac{1000}{45} = 0.0623 $  M Ans. (A)

Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: Hardness Measurement (ppm)
Content: Measurement of Hardness :
Hardness is measured in terms of ppm (parts per million) of  $ CaCO_{3} $  or equivalent to it.
Hardness in ppm =  $ \frac{mass of CaCO_{3}}{Total mass of solution} \times 10^{6} $

chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: Miscellaneous Problems: Normality Calculations (Q5-Q6)
Content: 5. Calculate the normality of a solution obtained by mixing 50 mL of 5 M solution of  $ K_{2}Cr_{2}O_{7} $  and 50 mL of 2 M  $ K_{2}Cr_{2}O_{7} $  in acidic medium.
Sol. v.f. of  $ K_{2}Cr_{2}O_{7}=6 $
$$ N_{f} = \frac{N_{1} V_{1} + N_{2} V_{2}}{V_{1} + V_{2}} = \frac{5 \times 6 \times 50 + 2 \times 6 \times 50}{50 + 50} = 21 N $$
6. Calculate the normality of a solution containing 13.4 g of Sodium oxalate in 100 mL Sol.
Sol. Normality =  $ \frac{wt. in g/eq. wt}{vol. of solution in litre} $
Here, eq. wt. of  $ Na_{2}C_{2}O_{4} = 134/2 = 67 $
so  $ N = \frac{13.4/67}{100/1000} = 2N $

combined_text:

Sol. v.f. of  $ K_{2}Cr_{2}O_{7}=6 $
$$ N_{f} = \frac{N_{1} V_{1} + N_{2} V_{2}}{V_{1} + V_{2}} = \frac{5 \times 6 \times 50 + 2 \times 6 \times 50}{50 + 50} = 21 N $$

chunks.summary: This chapter covers the fundamental concepts of chemical solutions, including their types, methods for expressing concentration (mass %, mole fraction, molarity, molality), gas solubility governed by Henry's Law, vapor pressure lowering and Raoult's Law, colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure), and the concept of abnormal molar masses using the van't Hoff factor.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Applications of Osmotic Pressure Measurement
Content: Measurement of osmotic pressure provides another method of determining molar masses of solutes. This method is widely used to determine molar masses of proteins, polymers and other
macromolecules. The osmotic pressure method has the advantage over other methods as pressure measurement is around the room temperature and the molarity of the solution is used instead of molarity. As compared to other colligative properties, its magnitude is large even for very dilute solutions. The technique of osmotic pressure for determination of molar mass of solutes is particularly useful for biomolecules as they are generally not stable at higher temperatures and polymers have poor solubility.

chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Abnormal Molar Masses Introduction (Association)
Content: $$ 2CH_{3}COOH\rightleftharpoons(CH_{3}COOH)_{2} $$
[Image Reference: Alt Text: "Image". Image Path: "images/CLASS-12/CHEMISTRY/Unit 1: Solutions/NCERT_Textbooks/img_in_image_box_124_302_432_412.jpg"]
Molecules of ethanoic acid (acetic acid) dimerise in benzene due to hydrogen bonding. This normally happens in solvents of low dielectric constant. In this case the number of particles is reduced due to dimerisation. Association of molecules is depicted as follows:
It can be undoubtedly stated here that if all the molecules of ethanoic acid associate in benzene, then  $ \Delta T_{b} $  or  $ \Delta T_{f} $  for ethanoic acid will be half of the normal value. The molar mass calculated on the basis of this  $ \Delta T_{b} $  or  $ \Delta T_{f} $  will, therefore, be twice the expected value. Such a molar mass that is either lower or higher than the expected or normal value is called as abnormal molar mass.

chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Osmotic Pressure Definition (Hydrostatic vs External)
Content: Osmotic Pressure :
[Image Reference: Alt Text: "Image". Image Path: "images/CLASS-12/CHEMISTRY/Unit 1: Solutions/Resonance/Solution and Colligative Properties/img_in_image_box_353_167_832_428.jpg"]
The equilibrium hydrostatic pressure developed by solution column when it is separated from solvent by semipermeable membrane is called osmotic pressure of the solution.
$$ \pi = \rho g h $$
ρ = density of solution
g = acceleration due to gravity
1 atm = 1.013 x 10^{5} N/m^{2}
h = eq. height
[Image Reference: Alt Text: "Image". Image Path: "images/CLASS-12/CHEMISTRY/Unit 1: Solutions/Resonance/Solution and Colligative Properties/img_in_image_box_238_493_799_840.jpg"]
D41 Osmotic Pressure: The external pressure which must be applied on solution side to stop the process of osmosis is called osmotic pressure of the solution.
If two solutions of concentration  $ C_{1} $  and  $ C_{2} $  are kept separated by SPM, and  $ C_{1} > C_{2} $  then particle movement take place from lower to higher concentration. So, extra pressure is applied on higher concentration side to stop osmosis.

combined_text:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Osmotic Pressure Definition (Hydrostatic vs External)
Content: Osmotic Pressure :
[Image Reference: Alt Text: "Image". Image Path: "images/CLASS-12/CHEMISTRY/Unit 1: Solutions/Resonance/Solution and Colligative Properties/img_in_image_box_353_167_832_428.jpg"]
The equilibrium hydrostatic pressure developed by solution column when it is separated from solvent by semipermeable membrane is called osmotic pressure of the solution.
$$ \pi = \rho g h $$
ρ = density of solution
g = acceleration due to gravity
1 atm = 1.013 x 10^{5} N/m^{2}
h = eq. height
[Image Reference: Alt Text: "Image". Image Path: "images/CLASS-12/CHEMISTRY/Unit 1: Solutions/Resonance/Solution and Colligative Properties/img_in_image_box_238_493_799_840.jpg"]
D41 Osmotic Pressure: The external pressure which must be applied on solution side to stop the process of osmosis is called osmotic pressure of the solution.
If two solutions of concentration  $ C_{1} $  and  $ C_{2} $  are kept separated by SPM, and  $ C_{1} > C_{2} $  then particle movement take place from lower to higher concentration. So, extra pressure is applied on higher concentration side to stop osmosis.

chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: EBP Theoretical Basis (Entropy Change)
Content: ## Extra Information (Not for Boards)
As only solvent particles are going into vapours,
We have:  $ \Delta H_{vapourisation of solvent} = \Delta H_{vapourisation of solution} $
Vapour is always pure solvent
$$ S_{vapors} $$
$$ S_{solvent} $$
$$ S_{solution} $$
$$ \Delta S_{vapourisation}=\frac{\Delta H_{vapourisation}}{T_{b,solvent}} $$
$$ \Delta S_{vapourisation of pure solvent} = \frac{\Delta H}{T_{b,solvent}} $$
$$ \Delta\mathsf{S}_{vapourisation of solution}=\frac{\Delta\mathsf{H}}{\mathsf{T}_{b,\text{solution}}} $$
Since,  $ \Delta S_{vapourisation of pure solvent} > \Delta S_{vapourisation of solution} $  ; So,  $ T_{b, solvent} < \Delta T_{b, solution} $
Due to presence of solute, it is difficult to vapourise the solution, i.e it is difficult to boil the solution. So, there is elevation in boiling point with respect to pure solvent.

Context: Solved Examples: EBP Calculations
Content: ## Solved Examples
Example-16: A solution of 122 g of benzoic acid in 1000 g of benzene shows a boiling point elevation of 1.4°. Assuming that solute is dimerized to the extent of 80 percent, calculate normal boiling point of benzene. Given molar enthalpy of vaporization of benzene = 7.8 Kcal/mole.
Solution :  $ T_{b}-T_{i}=\frac{122\times1000}{122\times1000}\times K_{b}\left(1+\left(\frac{1}{2}-1\right)\right) $
$$  T_{b}-T_{i}=K_{b}\times0.4 $$
$$ K_{b}=\frac{RT_{b}^{2}}{1000\times L_{vap.}} $$
$$ \mathsf{K_{b}}=\frac{2\times\mathsf{T_{b}}^{2}\times78}{1000\times7.8\times1000} $$
$$ 1.4=\frac{0.4\times2}{10^{5}}T_{b}^{2} $$
$$ T_{b}=418.33K $$
Example-17: 1 Lit. of aq. solution of urea having density = 1.060 g/mL is found to have $\Delta T_{b} = 0.5^{\circ}C$. If temperature of this solution increase to 101.5^{\circ}C, then calculate amount of water which must have gone in vapour state upto this pt. given $K_{b} = 0.5 K kg mol^{-1}$ for water
Solution : mass of solution = 1.060 × 10^{3} = 1060 g
0.5 = 0.5 m  $ \Rightarrow $  m = 1 ; if moles of urea = x
$$ 1=\frac{x}{1060-60x};x=1 $$
mass of water = 1060 - 60 = 1000 g
$$ 1.5=\Delta T_{b}=(molality)_{f}\times K_{b} $$
$$ (Molality)_{f}=3\quad\Rightarrow\quad3=1\times\frac{1000}{W_{water}} $$
mass of water vaporised = 1000 -  $ \frac{1000}{3} $  =  $ \frac{2000}{3} $  g = 666.67 g

combined_text:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: EBP Theoretical Basis (Entropy Change)
Content: ## Extra Information (Not for Boards)
As only solvent particles are going into vapours,
We have:  $ \Delta H_{vapourisation of solvent} = \Delta H_{vapourisation of solution} $
Vapour is always pure solvent
$$ S_{vapors} $$
$$ S_{solvent} $$
$$ S_{solution} $$
$$ \Delta S_{vapourisation}=\frac{\Delta H_{vapourisation}}{T_{b,solvent}} $$
$$ \Delta S_{vapourisation of pure solvent} = \frac{\Delta H}{T_{b,solvent}} $$
$$ \Delta\mathsf{S}_{vapourisation of solution}=\frac{\Delta\mathsf{H}}{\mathsf{T}_{b,\text{solution}}} $$
Since,  $ \Delta S_{vapourisation of pure solvent} > \Delta S_{vapourisation of solution} $  ; So,  $ T_{b, solvent} < \Delta T_{b, solution} $
Due to presence of solute, it is difficult to vapourise the solution, i.e it is difficult to boil the solution. So, there is elevation in boiling point with respect to pure solvent.

chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.context: Vapour Pressure and Boiling Point Relationship
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Vapour Pressure and Boiling Point Relationship
Content: (6) Saturated vapour pressure and boiling point:
A liquid boils when its saturated vapour pressure becomes equal to the external pressure on the liquid. When that happens, it enables bubbles of vapour to form throughout the liquid. If the external pressure is higher than the saturated vapour pressure, these bubbles are prevented from forming, and we just get evaporation at the surface of the liquid.
$ ^{*} $   $ P_{ext} > P_{sat} \Rightarrow evaporation $
$$ \mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{ext}}=\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{sat}}\Rightarrow\mathsf{Boiling} $$
If the liquid is in an open container and exposed to normal atmospheric pressure, the liquid boils when its saturated vapour pressure becomes equal to 1 atmosphere. This happens with water when the temperature reaches  $ 100^{\circ} $ C. But at different pressures, water will boil at different temperatures. For example, at the top of Mount Everest the pressure is so low that water will boil at about  $ 70^{\circ} $ C.

context: Example 1.10: Molar Mass Determination using Freezing Point Depression

chunks.summary: This chapter covers the fundamental concepts of chemical solutions, including their types, methods for expressing concentration (mass %, mole fraction, molarity, molality), gas solubility governed by Henry's Law, vapor pressure lowering and Raoult's Law, colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure), and the concept of abnormal molar masses using the van't Hoff factor.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.context: Example 1.10: Molar Mass Determination using Freezing Point Depression
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Example 1.10: Molar Mass Determination using Freezing Point Depression
Content: Example 1.10
1.00 g of a non-electrolyte solute dissolved in 50 g of benzene lowered the freezing point of benzene by 0.40 K. The freezing point depression constant of benzene is 5.12 K kg mol $ ^{-1} $ . Find the molar mass of the solute.
Solution
Substituting the values of various terms involved in equation (1.36) we get,
$$ M_{2}=\frac{5.12\ K\ kg\ mol^{-1}\times1.00\ g\times1000\ g\ kg^{-1}}{0.40\times50\ g}=256\ g\ mol^{-1} $$
Thus, molar mass of the solute = 256 g mol $ ^{-1} $

chunks.summary: This chapter covers the fundamental concepts of chemical solutions, including their types, methods for expressing concentration (mass %, mole fraction, molarity, molality), gas solubility governed by Henry's Law, vapor pressure lowering and Raoult's Law, colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure), and the concept of abnormal molar masses using the van't Hoff factor.

Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Example 1.9: Freezing Point Depression Calculation
Content: Example 1.9
45 g of ethylene glycol (C₂H₆O₂) is mixed with 600 g of water. Calculate (a) the freezing point depression and (b) the freezing point of the solution.
Solution
Depression in freezing point is related to the molality, therefore, the molality
of the solution with respect to ethylene glycol =  $ \frac{moles of ethylene glycol}{mass of water in kilogram} $
Moles of ethylene glycol =  $ \frac{45\ g}{62\ g\ mol^{-1}} = 0.73\ mol $
Mass of water in kg =  $ \frac{600g}{1000g kg^{-1}} = 0.6 kg $
Hence molality of ethylene glycol =  $ \frac{0.73 mol}{0.60 kg} = 1.2 mol kg^{-1} $
Therefore freezing point depression,
$$ \ddot{A}T_{\mathrm{f}}=1.86\mathrm{~K~kg~mol^{-1}}\times1.2\mathrm{~m~o~l~k~g^{-1}}=2.2\mathrm{~K~} $$
Freezing point of the aqueous solution = 273.15 K - 2.2 K = 270.95 K

Depression in freezing point is related to the molality, therefore, the molality

$$ \ddot{A}T_{\mathrm{f}}=1.86\mathrm{~K~kg~mol^{-1}}\times1.2\mathrm{~m~o~l~k~g^{-1}}=2.2\mathrm{~K~} $$
Freezing point of the aqueous solution = 273.15 K - 2.2 K = 270.95 K

chunks.context: Solved Example: Calculating Dissolved Gas Concentration using Henry's Law
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Solved Example: Calculating Dissolved Gas Concentration using Henry's Law
Content: ## Solved Examples
Example-7: Henry's law constant for  $ O_{2} $  dissolved in water is  $ 4.34 \times 10^{4} $  atm at  $ 25^{\circ}C $ . If the partial pressure of oxygen in air is 0.4 atm. Calculate the concentration (M) of dissolved oxygen in water in equilibrium with air at  $ 25^{\circ}C $ :
Solution : Given  $ K_{H} = 4.34 \times 10^{4} $  atm
$$ \mathsf{p}_{\mathsf{O}_{2}}=0.4\mathsf{a t m} $$
acc. to Henry's Law
$$ p=K_{H}X $$
$$ X_{O_{2}}=\frac{0.4}{4.34\times10^{4}}=9.2\times10^{-6} $$
Moles of water ( $ n_{H_{2}O} $ ) =  $ \frac{1000}{18} $  = 55.8 mol
$$ \mathsf{X}_{\mathsf{O}_{2}}=\frac{\mathsf{n}_{\mathsf{O}_{2}}}{\mathsf{n}_{\mathsf{O}_{2}}+\mathsf{n}_{\mathsf{H}_{2}\mathsf{O}}} $$
Since  $ n_{O_{2}} $  is very small in comparison to  $ n_{H_{2}O} $
$$ \mathsf{X}_{\mathsf{O}_{2}}=\frac{\mathsf{n}_{\mathsf{O}_{2}}}{\mathsf{n}_{\mathsf{H}_{2}\mathsf{O}}} $$
or  $ X_{O_{2}} \times n_{H_{2}O} = n_{O_{2}} $
So  $ n_{O_{2}} = 9.2 \times 10^{-6} \times 55.55 = 5.11 \times 10^{-4} $  mol
So  $ M = 5.11 \times 10^{-4} $

chunks.summary: This chapter covers the fundamental concepts of chemical solutions, including their types, methods for expressing concentration (mass %, mole fraction, molarity, molality), gas solubility governed by Henry's Law, vapor pressure lowering and Raoult's Law, colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure), and the concept of abnormal molar masses using the van't Hoff factor.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Freezing Point Depression Formulae
Content: Similar to elevation of boiling point, depression of freezing point ( $ \Delta T_{f} $ ) for dilute solution (ideal solution) is directly proportional to molality, m of the solution. Thus,
or
$$ \begin{aligned}\Delta T_{\mathrm{f}}\quad&\propto\quad\mathrm{m}\\\Delta T_{\mathrm{f}}&=K_{\mathrm{f}}\mathrm{~m}\end{aligned} $$
The proportionality constant,  $ K_{r} $ , which depends on the nature of the solvent is known as Freezing Point Depression Constant or Molal
Depression Constant or Cryoscopic Constant. The unit of  $ K_{f} $  is K kg mol $ ^{-1} $ . Values of  $ K_{f} $  for some common solvents are listed in Table 1.3.
If  $ w_{2} $  gram of the solute having molar mass as  $ M_{2} $ , present in  $ w_{1} $  gram of solvent, produces the depression in freezing point  $ \Delta T_{f} $  of the solvent then molality of the solute is given by the equation (1.31).
$$ \mathrm{m}\quad=\frac{w_{2}\ /M_{2}}{w_{1}/1000} $$
Substituting this value of molality in equation (1.34) we get:
$$ \begin{aligned}\Delta T_{\mathrm{f}}&=\frac{K_{\mathrm{f}}\times w_{2}/M_{2}}{w_{1}/1000}\\\Delta T_{\mathrm{f}}&=\frac{\dot{K}_{\mathrm{f}}\times w_{2}\times1000}{M_{2}\times w_{1}}\\M_{2}&=\frac{\dot{K}_{\mathrm{f}}\times w_{2}\times1000}{\Delta T_{\mathrm{f}}\times w_{1}}\end{aligned} $$
Thus for determining the molar mass of the solute we should know the quantities  $ w_{1} $ ,  $ w_{2} $ ,  $ \Delta T_{f} $ , along with the molar freezing point depression constant.

context: Elevation of Boiling Point Definition and Concept

chunks.summary: This chapter covers the fundamental concepts of chemical solutions, including their types, methods for expressing concentration (mass %, mole fraction, molarity, molality), gas solubility governed by Henry's Law, vapor pressure lowering and Raoult's Law, colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure), and the concept of abnormal molar masses using the van't Hoff factor.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.context: Elevation of Boiling Point Definition and Concept
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Elevation of Boiling Point Definition and Concept
Content: #### 1.6.2 Elevation of Boiling Point
The vapour pressure of a liquid increases with increase of temperature. It boils at the temperature at which its vapour pressure is equal to the atmospheric pressure. For example, water boils at 373.15 K (100°C) because at this temperature the vapour pressure of water is 1.013 bar (1 atmosphere). We have also learnt in the last section that vapour pressure of the solvent decreases in the presence of non-volatile solute. Fig. 1.7 depicts the variation of vapour pressure of the pure solvent and solution as a function of temperature. For example, the vapour pressure of an aqueous solution of sucrose is less than 1.013 bar at 373.15 K. In order to make this solution boil, its vapour pressure must be increased to 1.013 bar by raising the temperature above the boiling temperature of the pure solvent (water). Thus, the boiling point of a solution is ___.
[Image Reference: Alt Text: "Image". Image Path: "images/CLASS-12/CHEMISTRY/Unit 1: Solutions/NCERT_Textbooks/img_in_image_box_77_129_467_467.jpg"]
always higher than that of the boiling point of the pure solvent in which the solution is prepared as shown in Fig. 1.7. Similar to lowering of vapour pressure, the elevation of boiling point also depends on the number of solute molecules rather than their nature. A solution of 1 mol of sucrose in 1000 g of water boils at 373.52 K at one atmospheric pressure.
Let  $ T_{b}^{0} $  be the boiling point of pure solvent and  $ T_{b} $  be the boiling point of solution. The increase in the boiling point  $ \Delta T_{b} = T_{b} - T_{b}^{0} $  is known as elevation of boiling point.
### Fig. 1.7: The vapour pressure curve for solution lies below the curve for pure water. The diagram shows that  $ \Delta T_{b} $  denotes the elevation of boiling point of a solvent in solution.

chunks.context: Solved Example: Calculating pH from FPD (Weak Electrolyte)
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Solved Example: Calculating pH from FPD (Weak Electrolyte)
Content: Example-22 : Depression of freezing point of 0.01 molal aq. CH₃COOH solution is 0.02046°. 1 molal urea solution freezes at -1.86°C. Assuming molality equal to molarity, pH of CH₃COOH solution is :
(A) 2
(B) 3
(C) 3.2
(D) 4.2
## Solution :
$$ \Delta T_{f}=k_{f}\times m $$
$$ \mathsf{k}_{\mathsf{f}}=\frac{\Delta\mathsf{T}_{\mathsf{f}}}{\mathsf{m}}\ \overset{}{\underset{}{\mathsf{=}}}\frac{1.86}{1}\ \overset{}{\underset{}{\mathsf{=}}}1.86 $$
Now for  $ CH_{3}COOH $
$$ \Delta T_{f}=i k_{f}m $$
so  $ i=\frac{0.02046}{1.86\times0.01}=1.1 $
Now  $ i = 1 + \alpha $
$$ \alpha=1.1-1=0.1 $$
$$ \begin{array}{ccc}{{{\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOH}\quad\rightleftharpoons}}}&{{{\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COO}^{-}}}}&{{{+}}} \\{{{\mathrm{C}}}}&{{{0}}}&{{{0}}} \\{{{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}_{\alpha}}}}&{{{\mathrm{C}_{\alpha}}}}&{{{\mathrm{C}_{\alpha}}}}\end{array} $$
$$ \left[H^{+}\right]=C\alpha=0.01\times0.1=0.001 $$
$$  pH=3. $$

$$ \Delta T_{f}=i k_{f}m $$
so  $ i=\frac{0.02046}{1.86\times0.01}=1.1 $
Now  $ i = 1 + \alpha $

chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: HLP Solution Q8: Titration of KOH and Na2CO3 mixture
Content: 8. KOH + Na_{2}CO_{3}
m.eq. of KOH + m.eq. of Na₂CO₃ (v.f. = 1) = m.eq. of HCl (in presence of phenolphthalein)
$$ \begin{aligned}a\times1+b\times1&=15\times\frac{1}{20}\end{aligned} $$
∴ a + b = 0.75 ⋯(i) (in presence of phenolphthalein)
m.eq. of KOH + m.eq. of  $ Na_{2}CO_{3} $  (v.f. = 2) = m.eq. of HCl (in presence of methyl orange)
$$ 1\times a+2\times b=25\times\frac{1}{20} $$
∴  $ a + 2 \times b = 1.25 $  ...(ii) (in presence of methyl orange)
by solving (i) & (ii), a = 0.25 m.moles.
$$  mass of KOH=\frac{0.25}{1000}\times56=0.014g $$

context: Vaporization in a Closed Container and Equilibrium

chunks.summary: Comprehensive study guide covering solutions, concentration terms (mass %, Molarity, Molality, Normality, Mole Fraction, ppm), vapor pressure concepts (Raoult's Law, Henry's Law, Azeotropes), and colligative properties (RLVP, Boiling Point Elevation, Freezing Point Depression, Osmotic Pressure), including Vant'Hoff factor for non-ideal behavior.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.context: Vaporization in a Closed Container and Equilibrium
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Vaporization in a Closed Container and Equilibrium
Content: Now imagine what happens if the liquid is in a closed container. Common sense tells that water in a sealed bottle doesn't disappear over time. But, there is still constant evaporation from the surface. Particles continue to break away from the surface of the liquid - but this time they are trapped in the space above the liquid.
As the gaseous particles bounce around, some of them will hit the surface of the liquid again, and be trapped there. This is called as condensation. There will rapidly be an equilibrium set up in which the number of particles leaving the surface is exactly balanced by the number rejoining it. At equilibrium, rate of evaporation becomes equal to the rate of condensation.

chunks.summary: This chapter provides a comprehensive overview of the Equivalent Concept in Chemistry, covering definitions of equivalent weight, n-factor (valency factor) calculation for elements, acids, bases, and salts, and the concept of Normality. It extensively details various types of titrations, including acid-base, redox (Permanganate), and Iodometric/Iodimetric methods, emphasizing the Law of Equivalence. The chapter also addresses advanced topics like the drawbacks of the equivalent concept, volume strength of hydrogen peroxide, water hardness measurement, and oleum strength calculation, supported by numerous solved examples.

Content: 7. The number of moles of ferrous oxalate oxidised by one mole of  $ KMnO_{4} $  in acidic medium is :
(A) 5/2
(B) 2/5
(C) 3/5
(D) 5/3
Sol. Eq. of  $ FeC_{2}O_{4} = Eq. $  of  $ KMnO_{4} $
moles of  $ FeC_{2}O_{4} \times 3 =  $  moles of  $ KMnO_{4} \times 5 $
so, moles of  $ FeC_{2}O_{4} = 5/3 $  Ans. (D)
8. How many moles of  $ KMnO_{4} $  are needed to oxidise a mixture of 1 mole of each  $ FeSO_{4} $  &  $ FeC_{2}O_{4} $  in acidic medium?
(A) 4/5          (B) 5/4          (C) 3/4          (D) 5/3
Sol. Eq. of  $ KMnO_{4} = Eq.\ of\ FeSO_{4} + Eq.\ of\ FeC_{2}O_{4} $
moles of  $ KMnO_{4} \times 5 = moles\ of\ FeSO_{4} \times 1 + moles\ of\ FeC_{2}O_{4} \times 3 $
∴ moles of  $ KMnO_{4} = 4/5 $  Ans. (A)

chunks.summary: This chapter covers the fundamental concepts of chemical solutions, including their types, methods for expressing concentration (mass %, mole fraction, molarity, molality), gas solubility governed by Henry's Law, vapor pressure lowering and Raoult's Law, colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure), and the concept of abnormal molar masses using the van't Hoff factor.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Types of Solutions and Table 1.1
Content: ### 1.1 Types of Solutions
Solutions are homogeneous mixtures of two or more than two components. By homogenous mixture we mean that its composition and properties are uniform throughout the mixture. Generally, the component that is present in the largest quantity is known as solvent. Solvent determines the physical state in which solution exists. One or more components present in the solution other than solvent are called solutes. In this Unit we shall consider only binary solutions (i.e.,
consisting of two components). Here each component may be solid, liquid or in gaseous state and are summarised in Table 1.1.
### Table 1.1: Types of Solutions
| Type of Solution | Solute | Solvent | Common Examples |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Gaseous Solutions | Gas | Gas | Mixture of oxygen and nitrogen gases |
| Liquid | Gas | Chloroform mixed with nitrogen gas |
| Solid | Gas | Camphor in nitrogen gas |
| Liquid Solutions | Gas | Liquid | Oxygen dissolved in water |
| Liquid | Liquid | Ethanol dissolved in water |
| Solid | Liquid | Glucose dissolved in water |
| Solid Solutions | Gas | Solid | Solution of hydrogen in palladium |
| Liquid | Solid | Amalgam of mercury with sodium |
| Solid | Solid | Copper dissolved in gold |

chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Applications of Henry's Law
Content: Henry’s law finds several applications in industry and explains some biological phenomena. Notable among these are:
• To increase the solubility of  $ CO_{2} $  in soft drinks and soda water, the bottle is sealed under high pressure.
- Scuba divers must cope with high concentrations of dissolved gases while breathing air at high pressure underwater. Increased pressure increases the solubility of atmospheric gases in blood. When the divers come towards surface, the pressure gradually decreases. This releases the dissolved gases and leads to the formation of bubbles of nitrogen in the blood. This blocks capillaries and creates a medical condition known as bends, which are painful and dangerous to life.
To avoid bends, as well as, the toxic effects of high concentrations of nitrogen in the blood, the tanks used by scuba divers are filled with air diluted with helium (11.7% helium, 56.2% nitrogen and 32.1% oxygen).
- At high altitudes the partial pressure of oxygen is less than that at the ground level. This leads to low concentrations of oxygen in the blood and tissues of people living at high altitudes or climbers. Low blood oxygen causes climbers to become weak and unable to think clearly, symptoms of a condition known as anoxia.

chunks.summary: This chapter provides a comprehensive overview of the Equivalent Concept in Chemistry, covering definitions of equivalent weight, n-factor (valency factor) calculation for elements, acids, bases, and salts, and the concept of Normality. It extensively details various types of titrations, including acid-base, redox (Permanganate), and Iodometric/Iodimetric methods, emphasizing the Law of Equivalence. The chapter also addresses advanced topics like the drawbacks of the equivalent concept, volume strength of hydrogen peroxide, water hardness measurement, and oleum strength calculation, supported by numerous solved examples.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: Miscellaneous Problems: Available Chlorine Percentage (Q13)
Content: 13. Calculate the percentage of available chlorine in a sample of 3.55 g of bleaching powder which was dissolved in 100 mL of water. 25 mL of this solution, on treatment with KI and dilute acid, required 20 mL of 0.125 N sodium thiosulphate Sol.
Ans. 10%
Sol. CaOCl_{2} + H_{2}O \longrightarrow Ca(OH)_{2} + Cl_{2}
$ Cl_{2} + 2KI \longrightarrow 2KCl + I_{2} $
$$  I_{2}+2Na_{2}S_{2}O_{3}\longrightarrow Na_{2}S_{2}O_{6}+2NaI $$
In 25 mL solution, moles of  $ Na_{2}S_{2}O_{3}=\frac{20}{1000} \times \frac{0.125}{1}=25 \times 10^{-4} $
So, moles of  $ I_{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times $  moles of  $ Na_{2}S_{2}O_{3} $
$$ =\frac{1}{2}\times25\times10^{-4}=12.5\times10^{-4} $$
So, in 100 mL solution, moles of Cl_{2}= 4 × 12.5 × 10^{-4}= 50 × 10^{-4}
So, weight of Cl_{2}=50×10^{-4}×71 g
% of available Cl_{2} =  $ \frac{50\times10^{-4}\times71}{3.55} $   $ \times $  100 = 10%

chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Definitions of Vapour Pressures
Content: ### 4.2 Important Points related to vapour pressure :
D19 Vapour pressure of a pure liquid : The pressure exerted by the vapours over the liquid surface at equilibrium is called vapour pressure. It increases with the increase in temperature.
Vapour pressure of solution : The pressure exerted by the vapours of solvent 'A' and solute 'B' in equilibrium with the liquid phase is called the vapour pressure of solution.
D21 Partial vapour pressure : The pressure of vapours of a component ‘A’ over a solution of ‘A’ and ‘B’ is called partial vapour pressure of component A. It is denoted by  $ p_{A} $ .

chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Solved Example: OP with Multiple Dissociating Species
Content: Example-30  $ \frac{0.1 M urea}{(A)} $ ,  $ \frac{0.1 M NaCl}{(B)} $ ,  $ \frac{0.1 M BaCl_{2}}{(C)} $
(Calculating osmotic pressure when reaction is not taking place)
Example-31 : Calculate osmotic pressure of a solutions having 0.1 M NaCl & 0.2 M Na₂SO₄ and 0.5 M H₂A.
(Given : Weak acid is 20% dissociated at 300 K).
Solution :
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \pi = \pi_{\text{NaCl}} + \pi_{\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4} + \pi_{\text{HA}} \\
& = 0.1 \, \text{RT} \times 2 + 0.2 \, \text{RT} \times 3 + 0.5 \, \text{RT} \times 1.2 \\
& = 0.0821 \times 300 \, (0.2 + 0.6 + 0.6) = 34.482 \, \text{atm}.
\end{aligned}
$$
Example-32 : If 0.04 M Na₂SO₄ solutions at 300 K is found to be isotonic with 0.05 M NaCl (100 % dissociation) solutions. Calculate degree of dissociation of sodium sulphate ?
Solution :  $ i_{1} $   $ C_{1} $  RT =  $ i_{2} $   $ C_{2} $  RT
$ i_{1} $   $ C_{1} = i_{2} $   $ C_{2} $
0.04 (1 + 2α) = 0.05 × 2
α = 0.75 = 75%.

Context: n-factor Definitions
Content: 3.6 'n' - factor
(i) For oxidizing/reducing agents : no. of e $ ^{-} $  involved in oxidation/reduction half reaction per mole of oxidising agent /reducing agent.
e.g. :  $ 5e^{-} + 8H^{+} + MnO_{4}^{-} \rightarrow Mn^{2+} + H_{2}O $  n- factor = 5
(ii) For acid/ base reactions : no. of  $ H^{+} $  ions displaced/  $ OH^{-} $  ions displaced per mole of acid/ base.
e.g. : NaOH n - factor = 1  $ H_{2}SO_{4} $  n - factor = 2
(iii) For salt :
$$ \begin{aligned}n=Total charge on cations.\\ or\\ total charge on anions\end{aligned}\Big\}\begin{aligned}Simple salts\end{aligned} $$
e.g. :  $ \mathrm{Al}_{2}(\mathrm{SO}_{4})_{3} $  n - factor = charge on the cation = 2 x 3 = 6

chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Isotonic, Hypertonic, and Hypotonic Solutions
Content: Two solutions having same osmotic pressure at a given temperature are called isotonic solutions. When such solutions are separated by semipermeable membrane no osmosis occurs between them. For example, the osmotic pressure associated with the fluid inside the blood cell is equivalent to that of 0.9% (mass/volume) sodium chloride solution, called normal saline solution and it is safe to inject intravenously. On the other hand, if we place the cells in a solution containing more than 0.9% (mass/volume) sodium chloride, water will flow out of the cells and they would shrink. Such a solution is called hypertonic. If the salt concentration is less than 0.9% (mass/volume), the solution is said to be hypotonic. In this case, water will flow into the cells if placed in this solution and they would swell.

combined_text:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Isotonic, Hypertonic, and Hypotonic Solutions
Content: Two solutions having same osmotic pressure at a given temperature are called isotonic solutions. When such solutions are separated by semipermeable membrane no osmosis occurs between them. For example, the osmotic pressure associated with the fluid inside the blood cell is equivalent to that of 0.9% (mass/volume) sodium chloride solution, called normal saline solution and it is safe to inject intravenously. On the other hand, if we place the cells in a solution containing more than 0.9% (mass/volume) sodium chloride, water will flow out of the cells and they would shrink. Such a solution is called hypertonic. If the salt concentration is less than 0.9% (mass/volume), the solution is said to be hypotonic. In this case, water will flow into the cells if placed in this solution and they would swell.

Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Comparison of Positive Deviation Parameters
Content: Positive deviation solution
Negative deviation solution
(ii) The inter molecular interaction is weaker as interaction found in any one of the pure components.
The inter molecular interaction is more than the interaction found in any of the pure components
A.....A molecular interaction is Stronger than B.....B
$$ \begin{array}{c|c}{{{A.....B}}}&{{{molecular interaction}}}&{{{A.....A}}} \\{{{\hline\quad is more than}}}&{{{B.....B}}} \\\end{array} $$
(iii) There is increase in volume on mixing and absorption of heat.
There is decrease in volume on mixing and evolution of heat takes place on mixing
$$ \Delta V_{mix}=positive\quad\Delta H_{mix}=positive $$
$$ \Delta S_{mix}=+\mathrm{i}ve $$
$$ \Delta G_{\mathrm{mix}}=-\mathrm{i}\mathrm{ve} $$
Ex.
$$ \begin{aligned}&\Delta V_{mix}=-ve\quad&\Delta H_{mix}&=-ive\\&\Delta S_{mix}=+ive\quad&\Delta G_{mix}&=-ive\\&H_{2}O+HCOOH,&\quad&H_{2}O+CH_{3}COOH\\&H_{2}O+HNO_{3},&\quad&CHCl_{3}+CH_{3}OCH_{3}\end{aligned} $$
Ex.
$$ \mathsf{H}_{2}\mathsf{O}+\mathsf{CH}_{3}\mathsf{OH}, $$
$$  C_{2}H_{5}OH+Hexane $$
$ C_{2}H_{5}OH + Cyclohexane, CHCl_{3} + CCl_{4} $

chunks.context: Definitions: Aqueous, Non-aqueous, Miscible, Immiscible, Alloys
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Definitions: Aqueous, Non-aqueous, Miscible, Immiscible, Alloys
Content: D6 Aqueous solution : When solute is dissolved in water, it is called aqueous solution, e.g., sugar solution, ethanol in water.
D7 Non-aqueous solution: When solute is dissolved in solvent other than water, it is called non-aqueous solution, e.g., iodine dissolved in alcohol (Tincture of iodine).
D8 Miscible liquids : Those liquids which mix with each other and form homogeneous mixture are called miscible liquids.
D9 Immiscible liquids : Those liquids which do not mix with each other are called immiscible liquids.
D10 Alloys : Solid solutions of the two or more metals are called alloys. One of them can be a non-metal also.

Context: Solution Snippets for Match the Column
Content: O-परमाणु के मिलीमोल =  $ \left(H_{3}PO_{4}\right)_{2} $  के मिलीमोल) × 4 =  $ \left(\frac{4.9}{98} \times 1000\right) \times 4 = 200 $
P, + 5 ऑक्सीकरण अवस्था में है (अधिकतम = +5)
यह एक ऑक्सीकारक के साथ क्रिया नहीं करेगा क्योंकि P पहले ही अधिकतम ऑक्सीकरण अवस्था में है।
(C)  $ H_{2}C_{2}O_{4} $  के तुल्योंक =  $ \frac{4.5}{90} \times 2 = 0.1 $
O-परमाणु के मिलीमोल =  $ \left(H_{2}C_{2}O_{4}\right)_{2} $  के मिलीमोल) × 4 =  $ \left(\frac{4.5}{90} \times 1000\right) \times 4 = 200 $
C, + 3 ऑक्सीकरण अवस्था में है (अधिकतम = +4).
यह एक ऑक्सीकारक के साथ क्रिया कर सकता है तथा C, + 3 से + 4 में ऑक्सीकृत हो सकता है।
(D)  $ Na_{2}CO_{3} $  तो खुद ही क्षारीय होता है, अतः यह क्षार से क्रिया नहीं करेगा।
O-परमाणु के मिलीमोल =  $ \left(Na_{2}CO_{3}\right)_{2} $  के मिलीमोल) × 3 =  $ \left(\frac{5.3}{106} \times 1000\right) \times 3 = 150 $ .
C + 4 ऑक्सीकरण अवस्था में है (अधिकतम = +4).
यह एक ऑक्सीकारक के साथ क्रिया नहीं करेगा क्योंकि C पहले ही अधिकतम ऑक्सीकरण अवस्था में है।

Content: 1. Hydrogen peroxide acts both as an oxidising and as a reducing agent depending upon the nature of the reacting species. In which of the following cases  $ H_{2}O_{2} $  acts as a reducing agent in acid medium ?
[JEE(Main) 2014 Online (12-04-14), 4/120]
हाइड्रोजन परऑक्साइड अपचायक तथा उपचायक दोनों प्रकार से व्यवहार करता है और यह निर्भर करता है अभिक्रिया करने वाले स्पीशीज के स्वभाव पर। निम्न में से किसके साथ  $ H_{2}O_{2} $  अम्लीय माध्यम में अपचायक के रूप में क्रिया करता है ?
[JEE(Main) 2014 Online (12-04-14), 4/120]
(1*)  $ MnO_{4}^{-} $
(2)  $ Cr_{2}O_{7}^{2-} $
(3)  $ SO_{3}^{2-} $
(4) KI

chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: Solution/Answer for F-2 (Iron ppm Calculation)
Content: Ans. 126 ppm
Sol. millimoles of  $ K_{2}Cr_{2}O_{7}=25 \times 0.002=0.050 $
meq or millimoles of  $ Fe^{2+} $  present in water sample =  $ 25 \times 0.002 \times 6 - 7.5 \times 0.01 = 0.225 $
∴ Mass of  $ Fe^{2+} $  in water sample =  $ 0.225 \times 56 \times 10^{-3} = 12.6 \times 10^{-3} $  grams
assuming 100 mL water = 100 gram water
ppm =  $ \frac{12.6 \times 10^{-3}}{100} \times 10^{6} = 126 $  ppm
HIL:  $ K_{2}Cr_{2}O_{7} $  के मिली मोल =  $ 25 \times 0.002 = 0.050 $  ppm
$$ \frac{1}{7} $$
$$ \mathsf{Fe}^{2+}\frac{}{}\bar{\upeta} $$
$$  มิลลีมอล =25\times0.002\times6-7.5\times0.01=0.225 $$
$$ \begin{aligned} 氵 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+} 钅 \mathrm{ 枳 }=0.225\times56\times10^{-3}=12.6\times10^{-3} 钅 \mathrm{M}\end{aligned} $$
$$ \begin{aligned} 검尔 &=100\end{aligned} $$
$$ \mathrm{ppm}=\frac{12.6\times10^{-3}}{100}\times10^{6}=\ 126\;\mathrm{ppm}. $$

chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Example 1.12: Percentage Association Calculation
Content: 2 g of benzoic acid ( $ C_{6}H_{5}COOH $ ) dissolved in 25 g of benzene shows a depression in freezing point equal to 1.62 K. Molal depression constant for benzene is 4.9 K kg mol $ ^{-1} $ . What is the percentage association of acid if it forms dimer in solution?
Example 1.12
The given quantities are:  $ w_{2}=2~g;~K_{f}=4.9~K~kg~mol^{-1};~w_{1}=25~g $ ,
$$ \Delta T_{f}=1.62\ K $$
Solution
Substituting these values in equation  $ (1.36) $  we get:
$$ M_{2}=\frac{4.9\ K\ kg\ mol^{-1}\times2\ g\times1000\ g\ kg^{-1}}{25\ g\times1.62\ K}=241.98\ g\ mol^{-1} $$
Thus, experimental molar mass of benzoic acid in benzene is
$$  = 241.98 \, \text{g mol}^{-1}  $$
Now consider the following equilibrium for the acid:
$$ 2\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{COOH}\rightleftharpoons(\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{COOH})_{2} $$
If x represents the degree of association of the solute then we would have  $ (1 - x) $  mol of benzoic acid left in unassociated form and correspondingly  $ \frac{x}{2} $  as associated moles of benzoic acid at equilibrium. Therefore, total number of moles of particles at equilibrium is:
$$ 1-x+\frac{x}{2}=1-\frac{x}{2} $$
Thus, total number of moles of particles at equilibrium equals van't Hoff factor i.
$$  But i=\frac{Normal molar mass}{Abnormal molar mass} $$
$$ =\frac{122g mol^{-1}}{241.98g mol^{-1}} $$
or
$$ \frac{x}{2}=1-\frac{122}{241.98}=1-0.504=0.496 $$
or
$$ x\ =2\times0.496=0.992 $$
Therefore, degree of association of benzoic acid in benzene is 99.2%.

chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: Solution/Answer for A-2 (Salt Equivalent Weights)
Content: Ans. (a) E = 58.5 or E = 58.5 ; (b) E = 87 or E = 87 ; (c) E = 51.67 or E = 51.67
Sol. (a)  $ E = \frac{58.5}{1 \times 1} = 58.5 $  or  $ E = E_{Na^{+}} + E_{Cl^{-}} = 23 + 35.5 = 58.5 $
(b)  $ E = \frac{174}{2 \times 1} = 87 $  or  $ E = E_{K^{+}} + E_{SO_{4}^{2-}} = 39 + 48 = 87 $
(c)  $ E = \frac{310}{3 \times 2} = 51.67 $  or  $ E = E_{Ca^{2+}} + E_{PO_{4}^{3-}} = 20 + 31.67 = 51.67 $

combined_text:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: Solution/Answer for A-2 (Salt Equivalent Weights)
Content: Ans. (a) E = 58.5 or E = 58.5 ; (b) E = 87 or E = 87 ; (c) E = 51.67 or E = 51.67
Sol. (a)  $ E = \frac{58.5}{1 \times 1} = 58.5 $  or  $ E = E_{Na^{+}} + E_{Cl^{-}} = 23 + 35.5 = 58.5 $
(b)  $ E = \frac{174}{2 \times 1} = 87 $  or  $ E = E_{K^{+}} + E_{SO_{4}^{2-}} = 39 + 48 = 87 $
(c)  $ E = \frac{310}{3 \times 2} = 51.67 $  or  $ E = E_{Ca^{2+}} + E_{PO_{4}^{3-}} = 20 + 31.67 = 51.67 $

Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Case I: Electrolyte Dissociation (i vs alpha)
Content: Der.4 Case - I : Electrolyte dissociates
Relation between i &  $ \alpha $  (degree of dissociation) :
Let the electrolyte be  $ A_{x}B_{y} $
$ A_{x}B_{y} $  (aq.)  $ \longrightarrow $   $ xA^{y+} + yB^{x-} $
t = 0 C 0 0
$ t_{eq} $  C(1 -  $ \alpha $ )  $ xC_{\alpha} $   $ yC_{\alpha} $
Net concentration = C -  $ C_{\alpha} + xC_{\alpha} + yC_{\alpha} = C $  [1 + (x + y - 1)  $ \alpha $ ] = C [1 + (n - 1)  $ \alpha $ ].
$ n = x + y $
= no. of particles in which 1 molecule of electrolyte dissociates
$ i = \frac{C $  [1 + (n - 1)  $ \alpha $ ]
F23
$ i = 1 + (n - 1) \alpha $
e.g. NaCl (100% ionised), i = 2. ;  $ BaCl_{2} $  (100% ionised), i = 3. ;  $ K_{4}[Fe(CN)_{6}] $  (75% ionised), i = 4.

chunks.summary: This chapter covers the fundamental concepts of chemical solutions, including their types, methods for expressing concentration (mass %, mole fraction, molarity, molality), gas solubility governed by Henry's Law, vapor pressure lowering and Raoult's Law, colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure), and the concept of abnormal molar masses using the van't Hoff factor.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Boiling Point Elevation Formulae
Content: Experiments have shown that for dilute solutions the elevation of boiling point ( $ \Delta T_{b} $ ) is directly proportional to the molal concentration of the solute in a solution. Thus
$$ \Delta T_{\mathrm{b}}\propto\mathrm{~m~} $$
$$  or\quad\Delta T_{b}=K_{b}m $$
Here m (molality) is the number of moles of solute dissolved in 1 kg of solvent and the constant of proportionality,  $ K_{b} $  is called Boiling Point Elevation Constant or Molal Elevation Constant (Ebullioscopic Constant). The unit of  $ K_{b} $  is K kg mol $ ^{-1} $ . Values of  $ K_{b} $  for some common solvents are given in Table 1.3. If  $ w_{2} $  gram of solute of molar mass  $ M_{2} $  is dissolved in  $ w_{1} $  gram of solvent, then molality, m of the solution is given by the expression:
$$  m\quad=\frac{w_{2}/M_{2}}{w_{1}/1000}=\frac{1000\times w_{2}}{M_{2}\times w_{1}} $$
Substituting the value of molality in equation (1.30) we get
$$ \Delta T_{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{K_{\mathrm{b}}\times1000\times w_{2}}{M_{2}\times w_{1}} $$
$$ M_{2}=\frac{1000\times w_{2}\times K_{\mathrm{b}}}{\Delta T_{\mathrm{b}}\times w_{1}} $$
Thus, in order to determine  $ M_{2} $ , molar mass of the solute, known mass of solute in a known mass of the solvent is taken and  $ \Delta T_{b} $  is determined experimentally for a known solvent whose  $ K_{b} $  value is known.

Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: NSEC Q14: Volume of KMnO4 required to react with H2C2O4
Content: 14. Oxalic acid,  $ H_{2}C_{2}O_{4} $ , reacts with paramanganate ion according to the balanced equation  $ 5H_{2}C_{2}O_{4}(aq) + 2MnO_{4}^{-}(aq) + 6H^{+}(aq) \rightleftharpoons 2Mn^{2+}(aq) + 10CO_{2}(g) + 8H_{2}O(l) $ . The volume in mL of 0.0162 M  $ KMnO_{4} $  solution required to react with 25.0 mL of 0.022 M  $ H_{2}C_{2}O_{4} $  solution is [NSEC-2011] (A) 13.6 (B) 18.5 (C) 33.8 (D) 84.4

context: Effect of Temperature on Solid Solubility

chunks.summary: This chapter covers the fundamental concepts of chemical solutions, including their types, methods for expressing concentration (mass %, mole fraction, molarity, molality), gas solubility governed by Henry's Law, vapor pressure lowering and Raoult's Law, colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure), and the concept of abnormal molar masses using the van't Hoff factor.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.context: Effect of Temperature on Solid Solubility
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Effect of Temperature on Solid Solubility
Content: ## Effect of temperature
The solubility of a solid in a liquid is significantly affected by temperature changes. Consider the equilibrium represented by equation 1.10. This, being dynamic equilibrium, must follow Le Chateliers Principle. In general, if in a nearly saturated solution, the dissolution process is endothermic ( $ \Delta_{sol} H > 0 $ ), the solubility should increase with rise in temperature and if it is exothermic ( $ \Delta_{sol} H < 0 $ ) the solubility should decrease. These trends are also observed experimentally.

combined_text:

The solubility of a solid in a liquid is significantly affected by temperature changes. Consider the equilibrium represented by equation 1.10. These trends are also observed experimentally.

chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Osmosis and Semipermeable Membranes
Content: #### 1.6.4 Osmosis and Osmotic Pressure
There are many phenomena which we observe in nature or at home. For example, raw mangoes shrivel when pickled in brine (salt water); wilted flowers revive when placed in fresh water, blood cells collapse when suspended in saline water, etc. If we look into these processes we
### Fig. 1.9
Level of solution rises in the thistle funnel due to osmosis of solvent.
[Image Reference: Alt Text: "Image". Image Path: "images/CLASS-12/CHEMISTRY/Unit 1: Solutions/NCERT_Textbooks/img_in_image_box_344_1038_756_1397.jpg"]
find one thing common in all, that is, all these substances are bound by membranes. These membranes can be of animal or vegetable origin and these occur naturally such as pig's bladder or parchment or can be synthetic such as cellophane. These membranes appear to be continuous sheets or films, yet they contain a network of submicroscopic holes or pores. Small solvent
molecules, like water, can pass through these holes but the passage of bigger molecules like solute is hindered. Membranes having this kind of properties are known as semipermeable membranes (SPM).
Assume that only solvent molecules can pass through these semi-permeable membranes. If this membrane is placed between the solvent and solution as shown in Fig. 1.9, the solvent molecules will flow through the membrane from pure solvent to the solution. This process of flow of the solvent is called osmosis.
The flow will continue till the equilibrium is attained. The flow of the solvent from its side to solution side across a semipermeable membrane can be stopped if some extra pressure is applied on the solution. This pressure that just stops the flow of solvent is called osmotic pressure of the solution. The flow of solvent from dilute solution to the concentrated solution across a semipermeable membrane is due to osmosis. The important point to be kept in mind is that solvent molecules always flow from lower concentration to higher concentration of solution. The osmotic pressure has been found to depend on the concentration of the solution.

find one thing common in all, that is, all these substances are bound by membranes. These membranes can be of animal or vegetable origin and these occur naturally such as pig's bladder or parchment or can be synthetic such as cellophane. These membranes appear to be continuous sheets or films, yet they contain a network of submicroscopic holes or pores. Small solvent
molecules, like water, can pass through these holes but the passage of bigger molecules like solute is hindered. Membranes having this kind of properties are known as semipermeable membranes (SPM).
Assume that only solvent molecules can pass through these semi-permeable membranes. If this membrane is placed between the solvent and solution as shown in Fig. 1.9, the solvent molecules will flow through the membrane from pure solvent to the solution. This process of flow of the solvent is called osmosis.
The flow will continue till the equilibrium is attained. The flow of the solvent from its side to solution side across a semipermeable membrane can be stopped if some extra pressure is applied on the solution. This pressure that just stops the flow of solvent is called osmotic pressure of the solution. The flow of solvent from dilute solution to the concentrated solution across a semipermeable membrane is due to osmosis. The important point to be kept in mind is that solvent molecules always flow from lower concentration to higher concentration of solution. The osmotic pressure has been found to depend on the concentration of the solution.

Content: ## Example-14 :
[Image Reference: Alt Text: "Image". Image Path: "images/CLASS-12/CHEMISTRY/Unit 1: Solutions/Resonance/Solution and Colligative Properties/img_in_image_box_290_298_498_443.jpg"]
0.1 mole NaCl 100 ml H_{2}O Rigid wall 0.1 mole urea 100 ml H_{2}O
What is the final volume of both container.
Solution :  $ i_{1}C_{1}=i_{2}C_{2} $
$$ 0.1\times2\qquad0.1\times1 $$
$$ \overline{100+x}=\overline{100-x} $$
$$ 200-2x=100+x. $$
$$ x = 33.3 \, ml. $$
So, final volume of container containing NaCl = 133.3 ml.

Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Intext Questions 1.9 to 1.12
Content: ## I ntext Questions
1.9 Vapour pressure of pure water at 298 K is 23.8 mm Hg. 50 g of urea  $ \left(\mathrm{NH}_{2}\mathrm{CONH}_{2}\right) $  is dissolved in 850 g of water. Calculate the vapour pressure of water for this solution and its relative lowering.
1.10 Boiling point of water at 750 mm Hg is 99.63°C. How much sucrose is to be added to 500 g of water such that it boils at 100°C.
1.11 Calculate the mass of ascorbic acid (Vitamin C,  $ C_{6}H_{8}O_{6} $ ) to be dissolved in 75 g of acetic acid to lower its melting point by  $ 1.5^{\circ}C $ .  $ K_{f} = 3.9 K kg mol^{-1} $ .
1.12 Calculate the osmotic pressure in pascals exerted by a solution prepared by dissolving 1.0 g of polymer of molar mass 185,000 in 450 mL of water at  $ 37^{\circ} $ C.

chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Henry's Law Constant and Temperature Effect (Table 1.2)
Content: It is obvious from equation (1.11) that higher the value of  $ K_{H} $  at a given pressure, the lower is the solubility of the gas in the liquid. It can be seen from Table 1.2 that  $ K_{H} $  values for both  $ N_{2} $  and  $ O_{2} $  increase with increase of temperature indicating that the solubility of gases
### Table 1.2: Values of Henry's Law Constant for Some Selected Gases in Water
| Gas | Temperature/K | $ K_{H} $ /kbar | Gas | Temperature/K | $ K_{H} $ /kbar |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| He | 293 | 144.97 | Argon | 298 | 40.3 |
| $ H_{2} $ | 293 | 69.16 | $ CO_{2} $ | 298 | 1.67 |
| $ N_{2} $ | 293 | 76.48 | Formaldehyde | 298 | 1.83×10^{-5} |
| $ N_{2} $ | 303 | 88.84 |
| $ O_{2} $ | 293 | 34.86 | Methane | 298 | 0.413 |
| $ O_{2} $ | 303 | 46.82 | Vinyl chloride | 298 | 0.611 |
increases with decrease of temperature. It is due to this reason that aquatic species are more comfortable in cold waters rather than in warm waters.

context: HLP Solution Q11: Dilution and titration to find original normality

chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.context: HLP Solution Q11: Dilution and titration to find original normality
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: HLP Solution Q11: Dilution and titration to find original normality
Content: 11. Assuming new normality of original  $ H_{2}O_{2} $  solution = X
After dilution to 100 mL of 10 mL of this solution, new normality will be (say  $ X_{1} $ )
$$ \mathsf{X}\times10=\mathsf{X}_{1}\times100 $$
$$ \mathsf{X}_{1}=\frac{\mathsf{X}}{10} $$
10 mL of this dilute solution is titrated with 25 mL, 0.0245 M KMnO_{4} solution.
So,  $ N_{1}V_{1}=N_{2}V_{2} $
$$ \frac{X}{10}\times10=0.0245\times5\times25 $$
$$ X=3.0625N $$
So, volume strength of original  $ H_{2}O_{2} $  solution = X × 5.6 = 3.0625 × 5.6 = 17.15 V

After dilution to 100 mL of 10 mL of this solution, new normality will be (say  $ X_{1} $ )
$$ \mathsf{X}\times10=\mathsf{X}_{1}\times100 $$
$$ \mathsf{X}_{1}=\frac{\mathsf{X}}{10} $$
10 mL of this dilute solution is titrated with 25 mL, 0.0245 M KMnO_{4} solution.
So,  $ N_{1}V_{1}=N_{2}V_{2} $
$$ \frac{X}{10}\times10=0.0245\times5\times25 $$
$$ X=3.0625N $$
So, volume strength of original  $ H_{2}O_{2} $  solution = X × 5.6 = 3.0625 × 5.6 = 17.15 V

chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Characteristics of Henry's Law Constant (K_H)
Content: 6.3 Characteristics of Henry's Law constant ( $ K_{H} $ ).
(i) Unit same as those of pressure: torr or bar.
(ii) Different gases have different value of  $ K_{H} $  for the same solvent.
(iii) The  $ K_{H} $  value of a gas is different in different solvents and it increases with the increase in temperature.
(iv) Higher the value of  $ K_{H} $  of a gas, lower will be its solubility. Since,  $ x = \frac{p}{K_{H}} $ .
Plot of p Vs x is a straight line passing through the origin with slope equal to  $ K_{H} $ .

chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.context: Solved Example: Converting % w/w to g/L using Density
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Solved Example: Converting % w/w to g/L using Density
Content: —— Solved Examples
Example-1: If we have 6% w/w urea solution with density 1.060 g/mL, then calculate its strength in g/L ?
Solution :
6 g urea is present in 100 g solution.
6 g in  $ \frac{100}{1.060} $  mL
$ \frac{100}{1.060} $  mL → 6 g.
∴ 1000 mL =  $ \frac{6}{100} \times 1.060 \times 1000 = 10.6 \times 6 = 63.6 $

Equivalent Concept & Titration
3. Weight of organic compound = 29.5 mg
NH₃ + HCl → NH₄Cl
HCl (remaining) + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O
(1.5 m mole)
Total milimole of HCl = 2
mili mole of HCl used by NH₃ = 2 - 1.5 = 0.5
mili mole of NH₃ = 0.5
weight of NH₃ = 0.5 × 17 mg = 8.5 mg
weight of nitrogen =  $ \frac{14}{17} \times 8.5 $  mg = 7 mg
∴ % of Nitrogen in compound =  $ \frac{7}{29.5} \times 100 = 23.7\% $ .

### PART - II
1.  $ \overset{+6}{\underset{Cr_{2}O_{7}^{2-}}{C r_{2}O_{7}^{2-}+14H^{+}+6I^{-}\longrightarrowC r^{3+}+7H_{2}O+3I_{2}}} $
2. HCl reduces  $ MnO_{4}^{-} $  to  $ Mn^{2+} $  and itself oxidises to  $ Cl_{2} $ .

Equivalent Concept & Titration
3. Weight of organic compound = 29.5 mg
NH₃ + HCl → NH₄Cl
HCl (remaining) + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O
(1.5 m mole)
Total milimole of HCl = 2
mili mole of HCl used by NH₃ = 2 - 1.5 = 0.5
mili mole of NH₃ = 0.5
weight of NH₃ = 0.5 × 17 mg = 8.5 mg
weight of nitrogen =  $ \frac{14}{17} \times 8.5 $  mg = 7 mg
∴ % of Nitrogen in compound =  $ \frac{7}{29.5} \times 100 = 23.7\% $ .

chunks.summary: This chapter provides a comprehensive overview of the Equivalent Concept in Chemistry, covering definitions of equivalent weight, n-factor (valency factor) calculation for elements, acids, bases, and salts, and the concept of Normality. It extensively details various types of titrations, including acid-base, redox (Permanganate), and Iodometric/Iodimetric methods, emphasizing the Law of Equivalence. The chapter also addresses advanced topics like the drawbacks of the equivalent concept, volume strength of hydrogen peroxide, water hardness measurement, and oleum strength calculation, supported by numerous solved examples.

chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: Solved Example 15: Back Titration Calculation (Sulphur Content)
Content: ## Solved Examples
Ex-15. The sulphur content of a steel sample is determined by converting it to  $ H_{2}S $  gas, absorbing the  $ H_{2}S $  in 10 mL of 0.005 M  $ I_{2} $  and then back titrating the excess  $ I_{2} $  with 0.002 M  $ Na_{2}S_{2}O_{3} $ . If 10 mL  $ Na_{2}S_{2}O_{3} $  is required for the titration, how many milligrams of sulphur are contained in the sample?
$$ \mathrm{I}_{2}+2\mathsf{S}_{2}\mathsf{O}_{3}{}^{2-}\longrightarrow2\mathrm{I}^{-}+\mathsf{S}_{4}\mathsf{O}_{6}{}^{2-} $$
Sol. Used millimoles of  $ I_{2} $  = (m.moles of  $ I_{2} $  taken initially) -  $ \frac{mmoles of hypo used}{2} $
$$ =0.005\times10-0.002\times\frac{10}{2} $$

Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Definitions: Isotonic, Hypotonic, Hypertonic
Content: Type of solutions :
D43 (a) Isotonic solution: Two solutions having same osmotic pressure are considered as isotonic solution.
$ \pi_{1} = \pi_{2} $  (at same temperature)
D44 (b) Hypotonic & Hypertonic solutions: If two solutions 1 and 2 are such that $\pi_{2} > \pi_{1}$, then 2 is called hypertonic solution and 1 is called hypotonic solution.

Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Vapor Pressure of Immiscible Liquids
Content: 7.1 Completely Immiscible Liquids and Steam Distillation
For mixture of two completely immiscible liquids, each liquid exerts its own vapor pressure, independent of the other, and the total vapor pressure is the sum of the separate vapour pressures of the two components in the pure state at the given temperature.
Thus,  $ p_{A} = P_{A}^{0} $  &  $ p_{B} = P_{B}^{0} $
F17  $ P_{T}=P_{A}^{0}+P_{B}^{0};\frac{P_{A}^{0}}{P_{B}^{0}}=\frac{n_{A}}{n_{B}} $
where  $ n_{A} $  and  $ n_{B} $  are the numbers of moles of each component present in the vapor phase, which on condensing form distillate.
The ratio of A to B in the vapor in terms of the actual weights  $ w_{A} $  and  $ w_{B} $  is
## F18
$$ w_{A}\quad n_{A}M_{A}\quad p_{A}^{0}M_{A} $$
$$ w_{B}\quad n_{B}M_{B}\quad p_{B}^{0}M_{B} $$

$$ w_{B}\quad n_{B}M_{B}\quad p_{B}^{0}M_{B} $$

Valency factor = number of replacable  $ OH^{-} $  ions per base molecule.
| Ex-2. | NaOH, KOH |
| --- | --- |
| Sol. | v .f. → | 1 | 1 |
|   | Eq. wt. → | M/1 | M/1 |
|   | ○ Bases may be defined as the substances in which OH group is/are directly attached with group I elements (Li,Na, K,Rb,Cs), group II elements (Be, Mg,Ca,Ba ) or group III elements (Al, Ga,In,Tl), transition metals, non-metallic cations like PH4+, NH4+, etc. |

context: Example 1.5: Raoult's Law Calculation for Volatile Liquids

chunks.summary: This chapter covers the fundamental concepts of chemical solutions, including their types, methods for expressing concentration (mass %, mole fraction, molarity, molality), gas solubility governed by Henry's Law, vapor pressure lowering and Raoult's Law, colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure), and the concept of abnormal molar masses using the van't Hoff factor.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.context: Example 1.5: Raoult's Law Calculation for Volatile Liquids
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Example 1.5: Raoult's Law Calculation for Volatile Liquids
Content: Example 1.5
Vapour pressure of chloroform (CHCl_{3}) and dichloromethane (CH_{2}Cl_{2}) at 298 K are 200 mm Hg and 415 mm Hg respectively. (i) Calculate the vapour pressure of the solution prepared by mixing 25.5 g of CHCl_{3} and 40 g of CH_{2}Cl_{2} at 298 K and, (ii) mole fractions of each component in vapour phase.
Solution
(i) Molar mass of  $ CH_{2}Cl_{2}=12\times1+1\times2+35.5\times2=85 $  g mol $ ^{-1} $
Molar mass of CHCl_{3} = 12 × 1 + 1 × 1 + 35.5 × 3 = 119.5 g mol^{-1}
$$  Moles of CH_{2}Cl_{2}\quad=\quad\frac{40g}{85g mol^{-1}}=0.47mol $$
$$  Moles of CHCl_{3}\quad=\frac{25.5g}{119.5g mol^{-1}}=0.213mol $$
$$  Total number of moles=0.47+0.213=0.683mol $$
$$ x_{CH_{2}Cl_{2}}=\frac{0.47mol}{0.683mol}=0.688 $$
$$ x_{CHCl_{3}}=1.00-0.688=0.312 $$
Using equation (2.16),
$$ \begin{aligned}\boldsymbol{p}_{total}\quad&=\boldsymbol{p}_{1}^{0}+\left(\boldsymbol{p}_{2}^{0}-\boldsymbol{p}_{1}^{0}\right)\boldsymbol{x}_{2}=200+\left(415-200\right)\times0.688\\&=200+147.9=347.9mm Hg\end{aligned} $$
(ii) Using the relation (2.19),  $ y_{i} = p_{i} / p_{total} $ , we can calculate the mole fraction of the components in gas phase ( $ y_{i} $ ).
$$ p_{CH_{2}Cl_{2}}=0.688\times415mm Hg=285.5mm Hg $$
$$ p_{CHCl_{3}}=0.312\times200mm Hg=62.4mm Hg $$
$$ y_{CH_{2}Cl_{2}}=285.5mm Hg/347.9mm Hg=0.82 $$
$$ y_{CHCl_{3}}=62.4mm Hg/347.9mm Hg=0.18 $$
Note: Since,  $ CH_{2}Cl_{2} $  is a more volatile component than  $ CHCl_{3} $ ,  $ [p_{CH_{2}Cl_{2}}^{0} = 415 $  mm Hg and  $ p_{CHCl_{3}}^{0} = 200 $  mm Hg] and the vapour phase is also richer in  $ CH_{2}Cl_{2} $   $ [y_{CH_{2}Cl_{2}} = 0.82 $  and  $ y_{CHCl_{3}} = 0.18] $ , it may thus be concluded that at equilibrium, vapour phase will be always rich in the component which is more volatile.

context: Effect of Temperature on Gas Solubility

chunks.summary: This chapter covers the fundamental concepts of chemical solutions, including their types, methods for expressing concentration (mass %, mole fraction, molarity, molality), gas solubility governed by Henry's Law, vapor pressure lowering and Raoult's Law, colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure), and the concept of abnormal molar masses using the van't Hoff factor.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.context: Effect of Temperature on Gas Solubility
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Effect of Temperature on Gas Solubility
Content: ## Effect of Temperature
Solubility of gases in liquids decreases with rise in temperature. When dissolved, the gas molecules are present in liquid phase and the process of dissolution can be considered similar to condensation and heat is evolved in this process. We have learnt in the last Section that dissolution process involves dynamic equilibrium and thus must follow Le Chatelier's Principle. As dissolution is an exothermic process, the solubility should decrease with increase of temperature.

chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Solved Example: Calculating OP of Mixed Solutions
Content: ## Solved Examples
Example-24: If  $ V_{1} $  mL of  $ C_{1} $  solution +  $ V_{2} $  mL of  $ C_{2} $  solution are mixed together then calculate final concentration of solution and final osmotic pressure. If initial osmotic pressure of two solutions are  $ \pi_{1} $  and  $ \pi_{2} $  respectively?
Solution :  $ C_{f}=\frac{C_{1}V_{1}+C_{2}V_{2}}{V_{1}+V_{2}} $
$$ \pi_{1}=C_{1}RT,\ C_{1}=\left(\frac{\pi_{1}}{RT}\right);\pi_{2}=C_{2}RT,\ C_{2}=\left(\frac{\pi_{2}}{RT}\right) $$
$$ \pi=\left(\frac{\mathsf{C}_{1}\mathsf{V}_{1}+\mathsf{C}_{2}\mathsf{V}_{2}}{\mathsf{V}_{1}+\mathsf{V}_{2}}\right)\mathsf{R}\mathsf{T} $$
$$ \pi=\left(\frac{\pi_{1}\mathsf{V}_{1}+\pi_{2}\mathsf{V}_{2}}{\mathsf{V}_{1}+\mathsf{V}_{2}}\right) $$

Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Solved Example: OP with Complex Formation
Content: Example-33: If 6 g of  $ CH_{3}COOH $  is dissolved in benzene to make 1 litre at 300 K. Osmotic pressure of solution is found to be 1.64 atm. If it is known that  $ CH_{3}COOH $  in benzene forms a dimer. Calculate degree of association of acetic acid in benzene?
Solution :
[Image Reference: Alt Text: "Image". Image Path: "images/CLASS-12/CHEMISTRY/Unit 1: Solutions/Resonance/Solution and Colligative Properties/img_in_image_box_240_220_831_307.jpg"]
$$ i=1+\left(\frac{1}{n}-1\right)\beta. $$
$$ 1.64=0.0821\times300\times\left[1+\left(\frac{1}{n}-1\right)\beta\right]\times0.1 $$
$$ 1.64=0.0821\times300\left[1-\frac{\beta}{2}\right]0.1 $$
$$ \frac{1.64}{0.0821\times30}=\frac{2-\beta}{2} $$
$$ \frac{1.64}{2.46}=\frac{2-\beta}{2} $$
$$ 4=6-3\beta $$
$$ 3\beta=2 $$
$$ \beta=2/3 $$
(Calculating osmotic pressure when reaction is taking place)
Example-34: If 200 ml of 0.2 M BaCl₂ solution is mixed with 500 ml of 0.1 M Na₂SO₄ solution. Calculate osmotic pressure of resulting solutions?
## Solution :
$ BaCl_{2} + Na_{2}SO_{4} \rightarrow BaSO_{4} + 2NaCl $
0.04 moles 0.05 0 0
0 0.01 No effect 0.08
0.01 0.08
0.7 No effect 0.08
0.7 0.08
$$ \pi=\left(\mathrm{i}_{1}\mathrm{C}_{1}+\mathrm{i}_{2}\mathrm{C}_{2}\right)\mathrm{R}\mathrm{T}. $$
$$ =(3\times\frac{0.01}{0.7}+2\times\frac{0.08}{0.7})0.082\times300.=6.685atm. $$

context: Boiling Point and Steam Distillation Principle

chunks.summary: Comprehensive study guide covering solutions, concentration terms (mass %, Molarity, Molality, Normality, Mole Fraction, ppm), vapor pressure concepts (Raoult's Law, Henry's Law, Azeotropes), and colligative properties (RLVP, Boiling Point Elevation, Freezing Point Depression, Osmotic Pressure), including Vant'Hoff factor for non-ideal behavior.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.context: Boiling Point and Steam Distillation Principle
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Boiling Point and Steam Distillation Principle
Content: (i) A system of two immiscible liquids will boil when the total vapor pressure P is equal to the atmospheric pressure or the given external pressure. The boiling point of the mixture is thus lower than that of either constituent.
(ii) Further, since the total vapor pressure is independent of the relative amounts of the two liquids, the boiling point, and hence the composition of the vapor and distillate, will remain constant as long as the two layers are present. e.g. Water and Benzene has boiling point = 68.9°C.
But boiling point = H_{2}O (pure) C_{6}H_{6} (pure)
100℃ 80.2℃
Steam Distillation: The boiling point of a solution of two immiscible liquids is less than the individual boiling points of both the liquids. This concept is used in steam distillation.
A liquid (generally organic) that is immiscible with water, and that has a higher boiling point than water can be boiled (distilled) at a much lower temperature by passing steam through it. In this way, the organic liquid can be purified from impurities using steam distillation.

chunks.context: Solved Example: FPD with Complex Ionization
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Solved Example: FPD with Complex Ionization
Content: Example-21: A 0.001 molal solution of a complex represented as  $ Pt(NH_{3})_{4}Cl_{4} $  in water had a freezing point depression of 0.0054°C. Given  $ K_{f} $  for  $ H_{2}O = 1.86 molality^{-1} $ . Assuming 100% ionisation of the complex, write the ionisation nature and formula of complex.
## Solution :
$$ \begin{array}{r r r}{[\mathrm{Pt}(\mathrm{NH}_{3})_{4}\mathrm{Cl}_{\mathrm{n}}]\mathrm{Cl}_{(4-\mathrm{n})}\longrightarrow[\mathrm{Pt}(\mathrm{NH}_{3})_{4}\mathrm{Cl}_{\mathrm{n}}]^{+(4-\mathrm{n})}+(4-\mathrm{n})\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}&{}&\\ 1&0&0\\ 0&1&(4-\mathrm{n})\end{array} $$
Thus particles after dissociation = 4 - n + 1 = 5 - n
$$ (i)=5-n $$
$$ \Delta T_{f}=K_{f}^{\prime}\times $$
$$ 0.0054=1.86\times0.001\times(5-n) $$
$$ n=2.1\approx2(integer value) $$
$$ \mathrm{[Pt(NH_{3})_{4}Cl_{2}]Cl_{2}\longrightarrow[Pt(NH_{3})_{4}Cl_{2}]^{2+}+2Cl^{-}} $$

chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: RLVP Definition and Formula (Non-volatile Solute)
Content: Lowering in VP = P^{0} - P_{S} = \Delta P
and Relative lowering in Vapour Pressure =  $ \frac{\Delta P}{P^{0}} $
Raoult's law (For non-volatile solutes): The vapour pressure of a solution of a non-volatile solute is equal to the vapour pressure of the pure solvent at that temperature multiplied by its mole fraction. OR Relative Lowering in Vapour Pressure = mole fraction of the non volatile solute in solution.
Der.6  $ P_{S} = x_{solvent}P^{0} = (1 - x_{solute})P^{0} $
F25
$$  RLVP=\frac{\mathsf{P}^{0}-\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{s}}}{\mathsf{P}^{0}}=\mathsf{x}_{\mathsf{Solute}}=\frac{\mathsf{n}}{\mathsf{n}+\mathsf{N}} $$
where n = number of moles of non-volatile solute and N = number of moles of solvent in the solution.
$$ \frac{\mathsf{P}^{0}}{\mathsf{P}^{0}-\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{s}}}=\frac{\mathsf{n}+\mathsf{N}}{\mathsf{n}}=1+\frac{\mathsf{N}}{\mathsf{n}} $$
$$ \frac{\mathsf{N}}{\mathsf{n}}=\frac{\mathsf{P}^{0}-\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{s}}}{\mathsf{P}^{0}}\quad-1=\frac{\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{s}}}{\mathsf{P}^{0}-\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{s}}} $$
$$ \frac{\mathsf{P}^{0}-\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{s}}}{\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{s}}}=\frac{\mathsf{n}}{\mathsf{N}} $$
$$ \frac{\mathsf{P}^{0}-\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{s}}}{\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{s}}}=\frac{\mathsf{w}}{\mathsf{m}}\times\frac{\mathsf{M}}{\mathsf{W}}=\frac{\mathsf{w}}{\mathsf{m}}\times\frac{\mathsf{M}}{\mathsf{W}}\quad\times\frac{1000}{1000}=\frac{\mathsf{w}}{\mathsf{m}}\quad\times\frac{1000}{\mathsf{W}}\quad\times\frac{\mathsf{M}}{1000} $$
F26
$$ \frac{\mathsf{P}^{0}-\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{s}}}{\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{s}}}=\left(\mathsf{m o l a i t y}\right)\times\frac{\mathsf{M}}{1000} $$
where w and W = mass of non-volatile solute and volatile solvent respectively
m and M = molar mass of non-volatile solute and volatile solvent respectively
If solute gets associated or dissociated; $\frac{P^{0}-P_{s}}{P_{s}}=\frac{i.n}{N}$ that is $\frac{P^{0}-P_{s}}{P_{s}}=i\times(\text{molality})\times\frac{M}{1000}$

combined_text:

and Relative lowering in Vapour Pressure =  $ \frac{\Delta P}{P^{0}} $

OR Relative Lowering in Vapour Pressure = mole fraction of the non volatile solute in solution.
Der.6  $ P_{S} = x_{solvent}P^{0} = (1 - x_{solute})P^{0} $
F25
$$  RLVP=\frac{\mathsf{P}^{0}-\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{s}}}{\mathsf{P}^{0}}=\mathsf{x}_{\mathsf{Solute}}=\frac{\mathsf{n}}{\mathsf{n}+\mathsf{N}} $$
where n = number of moles of non-volatile solute and N = number of moles of solvent in the solution.
$$ \frac{\mathsf{P}^{0}}{\mathsf{P}^{0}-\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{s}}}=\frac{\mathsf{n}+\mathsf{N}}{\mathsf{n}}=1+\frac{\mathsf{N}}{\mathsf{n}} $$
$$ \frac{\mathsf{N}}{\mathsf{n}}=\frac{\mathsf{P}^{0}-\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{s}}}{\mathsf{P}^{0}}\quad-1=\frac{\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{s}}}{\mathsf{P}^{0}-\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{s}}} $$
$$ \frac{\mathsf{P}^{0}-\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{s}}}{\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{s}}}=\frac{\mathsf{n}}{\mathsf{N}} $$
$$ \frac{\mathsf{P}^{0}-\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{s}}}{\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{s}}}=\frac{\mathsf{w}}{\mathsf{m}}\times\frac{\mathsf{M}}{\mathsf{W}}=\frac{\mathsf{w}}{\mathsf{m}}\times\frac{\mathsf{M}}{\mathsf{W}}\quad\times\frac{1000}{1000}=\frac{\mathsf{w}}{\mathsf{m}}\quad\times\frac{1000}{\mathsf{W}}\quad\times\frac{\mathsf{M}}{1000} $$
F26
$$ \frac{\mathsf{P}^{0}-\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{s}}}{\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{s}}}=\left(\mathsf{m o l a i t y}\right)\times\frac{\mathsf{M}}{1000} $$
where w and W = mass of non-volatile solute and volatile solvent respectively
m and M = molar mass of non-volatile solute and volatile solvent respectively
If solute gets associated or dissociated; $\frac{P^{0}-P_{s}}{P_{s}}=\frac{i.n}{N}$ that is $\frac{P^{0}-P_{s}}{P_{s}}=i\times(\text{molality})\times\frac{M}{1000}$

chunks.summary: This chapter covers the fundamental concepts of chemical solutions, including their types, methods for expressing concentration (mass %, mole fraction, molarity, molality), gas solubility governed by Henry's Law, vapor pressure lowering and Raoult's Law, colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure), and the concept of abnormal molar masses using the van't Hoff factor.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.context: Solubility of a Gas in a Liquid and Pressure Effect
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Solubility of a Gas in a Liquid and Pressure Effect
Content: #### 1.3.2 Solubility of a Gas in a Liquid
Many gases dissolve in water. Oxygen dissolves only to a small extent in water. It is this dissolved oxygen which sustains all aquatic life. On the other hand, hydrogen chloride gas (HCl) is highly soluble in water. Solubility of gases in liquids is greatly affected by pressure and
temperature. The solubility of gases increases with an increase of pressure. For solution of gases in a solvent, a system as shown in Fig. 1.1 (a). The lower part is solution and the upper part is a gaseous system at a pressure p and a temperature T. Assume this system to be in a state of dynamic equilibrium, i.e., under these conditions, the gaseous particles entering and leaving the solution phase is the same. Now, increase the pressure over the solution phase by compressing the gas to a smaller volume [Fig. 1.1 (b)]. This will increase the number of gaseous particles per unit volume over the solution and also the rate at which the gaseous particles are striking the surface of the solution to enter it. The solubility of the gas will increase until a new equilibrium is reached, resulting in an increase in the pressure of a gas above the solution and thus its solubility increases.
[Image Reference: Alt Text: "Image". Image Path: "images/CLASS-12/CHEMISTRY/Unit 1: Solutions/NCERT_Textbooks/img_in_chart_box_82_468_345_717.jpg"]
[Image Reference: Alt Text: "Image". Image Path: "images/CLASS-12/CHEMISTRY/Unit 1: Solutions/NCERT_Textbooks/img_in_chart_box_350_466_679_740.jpg"]
### Fig. 1.1: Effect of pressure on the solubility of a gas. The concentration of dissolved gas is proportional to the pressure on the gas above the solution.

chunks.summary: This chapter provides a comprehensive overview of the Equivalent Concept in Chemistry, covering definitions of equivalent weight, n-factor (valency factor) calculation for elements, acids, bases, and salts, and the concept of Normality. It extensively details various types of titrations, including acid-base, redox (Permanganate), and Iodometric/Iodimetric methods, emphasizing the Law of Equivalence. The chapter also addresses advanced topics like the drawbacks of the equivalent concept, volume strength of hydrogen peroxide, water hardness measurement, and oleum strength calculation, supported by numerous solved examples.

Content: 4. In an experiment, 50 mL of 0.1 M solution of a salt reacted with 25 mL of 0.1 M solution of sodium sulphite. The half equation for the oxidation of sulphite ion is :
$$  SO_{3}{}^{2-}\left(aq\right)+H_{2}O\longrightarrow SO_{4}{}^{2-}\left(aq\right)+2H^{+}+2e^{-} $$
If the oxidation number of metal in the salt was 3, what would be the new oxidation number of metal :

$$  SO_{3}{}^{2-}\left(aq\right)+H_{2}O\longrightarrow SO_{4}{}^{2-}\left(aq\right)+2H^{+}+2e^{-} $$

Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: Solution/Answer for Ex 2, Q14
Content: Sol. Valency factor of  $ K_{2}C_{2}O_{4} $ .  $ 3H_{2}C_{2}O_{4} $ .  $ 4H_{2}O $  is  $ 2 + 3(2) = 8 $
(as we now that  $ KMnO_{4} $  oxidises only  $ C_{2}O_{4}^{2-} $  to  $ CO_{2} $ )
Now equivalent of  $ K_{2}C_{2}O_{4} $ .  $ 3H_{2}C_{2}O_{4} $ .  $ 4H_{2}O $  = equivalent of  $ MnO_{4}^{-} $
$$
\frac{5.08}{508} \times 8 = 1 \times 5 \times V \times \frac{1}{1000}
$$
so V = 16 mL Ans.

chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: JEE Advanced Solution Q20: Total equivalents in mixed base titration
Content: 20. Equivalent of HCl = Equivalent of  $ (\mathrm{NaOH} + \mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{CO}_{3} + \mathrm{NOHCO}_{3}) $
$ 1 \times V = [x \times 10^{-3} \times 1] + [Y \times 10^{-3} \times 2] + [Z \times 10^{-3} \times 1] $
$ V = [x + 2Y + Z] \times 10^{-3} L $
$ V = [X + 2Y + Z] ml $

Context: Solution/Answer for Ex 2, Q7
Content: Sol. (A) 1 N (B) 0.5 N (C) 1.8 N (D*) 0.8 N
Eq. of  $ Na_{2}CO_{3} = Eq. $  of  $ H_{2}SO_{4} $
$ \frac{1.06}{106} \times 2 = \frac{25}{1000} \times N $
N = 0.8 N
