Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: Solution/Answer for Ex 2, Q1
Content: <fcel>1.<fcel>The equivalent weight of a metal is double that of oxygen. How many times is the weight of its oxide greater than weight of the metal?<lcel><lcel><nl><ucel><fcel>(A*) 1.5 (B) 2<fcel>(C) 0.5<fcel>(D) 3<nl><fcel>Sol.<fcel>Given  $ E_{\text{metal}} = 2 \times 8 = 16 $ <lcel><lcel><nl><ucel><fcel> $ \frac{\text{Weight}_{\text{oxide}}}{\text{Weight}_{\text{metal}}} = ? $ <lcel><lcel><nl><ucel><fcel> $ eq_{\text{metal}} = eq_{\text{oxide}} $ <lcel><lcel><nl><ucel><fcel> $ \frac{W_{\text{metal}}}{16} = \frac{W_{\text{oxide}}}{16 + 8} $ <lcel><lcel><nl><ucel><fcel>∴  $ \frac{W_{\text{oxide}}}{W_{\text{metal}}} = \frac{24}{16} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5 $ <lcel><lcel><nl><fcel>H.<fcel>Dिया गया है :  $ E_{\text{d缸}} = 2 \times 8 = 16 $ <lcel><lcel><nl><ucel><fcel> $ \frac{\text{B}_{\text{D缸}} - 0.5 - 0.5}{B_{\text{D缸}}} = ? $ <lcel><lcel><nl><ucel><fcel> $ eq_{\text{d缸}} = eq_{\text{D缸}} - 0.5 - 0.5 $ <lcel><lcel><nl><ucel><fcel> $ \frac{W_{\text{d缸}}}{16} = \frac{W_{\text{D缸}} - 0.5 - 0.5}{16 + 8} $ <lcel><lcel><nl><ucel><fcel>∴  $ \frac{W_{\text{D缸}} - 0.5 - 0.5}{W_{\text{d缸}}} = \frac{24}{16} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5 $ <lcel><lcel><nl>

Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Henry's Law Statement and Formula
Content: 6.2 Henry's Law (effect of pressure on solubility of gases in liquids) :
Statement : The solubility of a gas in a liquid at a given temperature is directly proportional to its partial pressure at which it is dissolved.
| Der.2 Let | x = Mole fraction of unreacted gas in solution at a given temperature as a measure of its solubility.p = Partial pressure of gas in equilibrium with the solution.By Henry's law: x ∝ p or p ∝ xThat is; F16 p = K_Hx or x = \frac{p}{K_H}, where K_H = Henry's law constant. |
| --- | --- |

chunks.context: Solubility of Solid in Liquid: Like Dissolves Like
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Solubility of Solid in Liquid: Like Dissolves Like
Content: ### 5.1 Solubility of a solid in a liquid :
Like dissolves like: Polar solutes dissolve in polar solvents and non polar solutes in non-polar solvents.
Sodium chloride and sugar dissolve readily in water, naphthalene and anthracene do not. On the other hand, naphthalene and anthracene dissolve readily in benzene but sodium chloride and sugar do not.

Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: Solution/Answer for A-3.1 (Metal Equivalent Weight Calculation)
Content: Ans. 20.1
Sol. Mass of Cl_{2} =  $ \frac{1.12}{22.4} $  × 71 = 3.55 g
3.55 g Cl = (5.56 - 3.55) g metal
35.5 g Cl = 20.1 g metal (equivalent mass of metal)
हल Cl₂ का द्रव्यमान =  $ \frac{1.12}{22.4} \times 71 = 3.55 $  ग्राम
3.55 ग्राम Cl ≡ (5.56 − 3.55) ग्राम धातु
35.5 ग्राम Cl ≡ 20.1 ग्राम धातु (धातु का तुल्यांकी द्रव्यमान)

context: Example 1.11: Molar Mass Determination using Osmotic Pressure

chunks.summary: This chapter covers the fundamental concepts of chemical solutions, including their types, methods for expressing concentration (mass %, mole fraction, molarity, molality), gas solubility governed by Henry's Law, vapor pressure lowering and Raoult's Law, colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure), and the concept of abnormal molar masses using the van't Hoff factor.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.context: Example 1.11: Molar Mass Determination using Osmotic Pressure
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Example 1.11: Molar Mass Determination using Osmotic Pressure
Content: ### Example 1.11
200 cm $ ^{3} $  of an aqueous solution of a protein contains 1.26 g of the protein. The osmotic pressure of such a solution at 300 K is found to be  $ 2.57 \times 10^{-3} $  bar. Calculate the molar mass of the protein.
Solution The various quantities known to us are as follows:  $ \Pi = 2.57 \times 10^{-3} $  bar,
$$ V=200\ cm^{3}=0.200\ litre $$
$$ T=300K $$
$$ \mathrm{R}=0.083\mathrm{~L~bar~mol^{-1}K^{-1}} $$
Substituting these values in equation (2.42) we get
$$ M_{2}=\frac{1.26\ g\times0.083\ L\ bar\ K^{-1}\ mol^{-1}\times300\ K}{2.57\times10^{-3}bar\times0.200\ L}=61,022\ g\ mol^{-1} $$

combined_text:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Example 1.11: Molar Mass Determination using Osmotic Pressure

200 cm $ ^{3} $  of an aqueous solution of a protein contains 1.26 g of the protein. The osmotic pressure of such a solution at 300 K is found to be  $ 2.57 \times 10^{-3} $  bar. Calculate the molar mass of the protein.
Solution The various quantities known to us are as follows:  $ \Pi = 2.57 \times 10^{-3} $  bar,
$$ V=200\ cm^{3}=0.200\ litre $$
$$ T=300K $$
$$ \mathrm{R}=0.083\mathrm{~L~bar~mol^{-1}K^{-1}} $$
Substituting these values in equation (2.42) we get
$$ M_{2}=\frac{1.26\ g\times0.083\ L\ bar\ K^{-1}\ mol^{-1}\times300\ K}{2.57\times10^{-3}bar\times0.200\ L}=61,022\ g\ mol^{-1} $$

Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: Solution/Answer for C-1 (Precipitation Equivalent Weight)
Content: Ans. 72.61
Sol. 72.01
Metal
sulphate (aq)  $ \xrightarrow{+BaCl_{2}} $   $ \xrightarrow{BaSO_{4}} $   $ \downarrow $
(0.95 g)
Now, 233.34 g BaSO_{4} = 96 g SO_{4}^{2-}
∴ 0.95 g BaSO_{4} =  $ \frac{96}{233.34} $   $ \times $  0.95 or 0.39 g SO_{4}^{2-}
∴ Mass of metal in metal sulphate = 0.98 - 0.39 = 0.59 g
Now, eq_{metal} = eq_{SO_{4}^{2-}}
$ \frac{0.59}{E_{metal}} = \frac{0.39}{96/2} $
$ E_{metal} = 72.61 $
$ \frac{0.59}{0.98\ g} + \frac{0.39}{0.95\ g} \xrightarrow{\text{BaSO}_4} $
$ \frac{0.59}{0.98\ g} + \frac{0.39}{0.95\ g} \xrightarrow{\text{BaSO}_4} $
$ \frac{0.59}{0.98\ g} + \frac{0.39}{0.95\ g} \xrightarrow{\text{BaSO}_4} $
$ \frac{0.59}{0.98\ g} + \frac{0.39}{0.95\ g} \xrightarrow{\text{BaSO}_4} $
$ \frac{0.59}{0.98\ g} + \frac{0.39}{0.95\ g} \xrightarrow{\text{BaSO}_4} $

Context: Reverse Osmosis and Measurement
Content: D42 Reverse Osmosis :
If the pressure applied on the solution side is more than osmotic pressure of the solution then the solvent particles will move from solution to solvent side. This process is known as reverse osmosis.
Berkely: Hartely device/method uses the above pressure to measure osmotic pressure.
e.g. used in desalination of sea-water.

F15 (b) Relative Humidity (R.H.) =  $ \frac{\text{Partial pressure of water vapour at given temperature}}{\text{Vapour pressure of water at the same temperature}} $  × 100%

chunks.summary: This chapter covers the fundamental concepts of chemical solutions, including their types, methods for expressing concentration (mass %, mole fraction, molarity, molality), gas solubility governed by Henry's Law, vapor pressure lowering and Raoult's Law, colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure), and the concept of abnormal molar masses using the van't Hoff factor.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Example 1.4: Henry's Law Application
Content: Example 1.4 If  $ N_{2} $  gas is bubbled through water at 293 K, how many millimoles of  $ N_{2} $  gas would dissolve in 1 litre of water? Assume that  $ N_{2} $  exerts a partial pressure of 0.987 bar. Given that Henry's law constant for  $ N_{2} $  at 293 K is 76.48 kbar.
Solution The solubility of gas is related to the mole fraction in aqueous solution. The mole fraction of the gas in the solution is calculated by applying Henry's law. Thus:
$$ x\left(Nitrogen\right)=\frac{p\left(nitrogen\right)}{K_{H}}=\frac{0.987bar}{76,480bar}=1.29\times10^{-5} $$
As 1 litre of water contains 55.5 mol of it, therefore if n represents number of moles of  $ N_{2} $  in solution,
$$ x\left(Nitrogen\right)=\frac{n\ mol}{n\ mol+55.5\ mol}=\frac{n}{55.5}=1.29\times10^{-5} $$
(n in denominator is neglected as it is  $ \langle\langle55.5\rangle $ )
Thus  $ n = 1.29 \times 10^{-5} \times 55.5 \, mol = 7.16 \times 10^{-4} \, mol $
$$ =\frac{7.16\times10^{-4}mol\times1000mmol}{1mol}=0.716mmol $$

combined_text:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic:

Solution The solubility of gas is related to the mole fraction in aqueous solution. The mole fraction of the gas in the solution is calculated by applying Henry's law. Thus:
$$ x\left(Nitrogen\right)=\frac{p\left(nitrogen\right)}{K_{H}}=\frac{0.987bar}{76,480bar}=1.29\times10^{-5} $$
As 1 litre of water contains 55.5 mol of it, therefore if n represents number of moles of  $ N_{2} $  in solution,
$$ x\left(Nitrogen\right)=\frac{n\ mol}{n\ mol+55.5\ mol}=\frac{n}{55.5}=1.29\times10^{-5} $$
(n in denominator is neglected as it is  $ \langle\langle55.5\rangle $ )
Thus  $ n = 1.29 \times 10^{-5} \times 55.5 \, mol = 7.16 \times 10^{-4} \, mol $
$$ =\frac{7.16\times10^{-4}mol\times1000mmol}{1mol}=0.716mmol $$

chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Mass Percentage Definition and Example
Content: (i) Mass percentage (w/w): The mass percentage of a component of a solution is defined as:
Mass % of a component
$$ \frac{Mass of the component in the solution}{Total mass of the solution}\times100 $$
For example, if a solution is described by 10% glucose in water by mass, it means that 10 g of glucose is dissolved in 90 g of water resulting in a 100 g solution. Concentration described by mass percentage is commonly used in industrial chemical applications. For example, commercial bleaching solution contains 3.62 mass percentage of sodium hypochlorite in water.

अमீयि माध्यम में आकसैलेट की परमैंगनेट के साथ अभिक्रिया में,  $ CO_{2} $  के एक अणु को बनाने में निहित इलेक्ट्रॉनों की संख्या है:
[JEE(Main) 2019 Online (10-01-19), 4/120]
(1) 5
(2*) 1
(3) 2
(4) 10

chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: MSP: Henry's Law Application
Content: 7. A 500 gm liquid consist of 15 gm ethane at any temp. T, at a pressure = 2 atm. Find Pressure of gas required to dissolve 30 gm gas in 300 gm liquid.
Ans.  $ P_{2}=6.66\ atm. $
Sol.  $ \frac{15/500}{30/300} = \frac{2}{P} \Rightarrow \frac{3}{10} = \frac{2}{P} \Rightarrow P = 6.66\ atm. $

context: Vapor Phase Composition in Liquid Solutions

chunks.summary: This chapter covers the fundamental concepts of chemical solutions, including their types, methods for expressing concentration (mass %, mole fraction, molarity, molality), gas solubility governed by Henry's Law, vapor pressure lowering and Raoult's Law, colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure), and the concept of abnormal molar masses using the van't Hoff factor.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.context: Vapor Phase Composition in Liquid Solutions
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Vapor Phase Composition in Liquid Solutions
Content: The composition of vapour phase in equilibrium with the solution is determined by the partial pressures of the components. If  $ y_{1} $  and  $ y_{2} $  are the mole fractions of the
components 1 and 2 respectively in the vapour phase then, using Dalton's law of partial pressures:
$$ p_{1}=y_{1}p_{total} $$
$$ p_{2}=y_{2}p_{total} $$
In general
$$ p_{\mathrm{i}}=y_{\mathrm{i}}\quad p_{\mathrm{total}} $$

$$ p_{1}=y_{1}p_{total} $$
$$ p_{2}=y_{2}p_{total} $$
In general
$$ p_{\mathrm{i}}=y_{\mathrm{i}}\quad p_{\mathrm{total}} $$

Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Solved Example: ppm to Molarity
Content: Example-5 : Calculate molarity of  $ CaCO_{3}(aq) $  solution which has concentration of  $ CaCO_{3}=200ppm $ .
Solution : 200 g of CaCO₃ in 10⁶ g of water.
$\frac{200}{100}=2$ moles of CaCO$_{3}$ in 10$^{3}$ liters of water. (density =1g/mL)
$$ \mathrm{So molarity}=\quad\frac{2}{10^{3}}=2\times10^{-3}\mathrm{M}. $$

chunks.context: Definition of Normality and Related Formulas
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Definition of Normality and Related Formulas
Content: 3.5 D16 Normality: It is defined as number of gram equivalents of solute dissolved per litre of solution.
F5 No. of equivalents per litre of solution =  $ \frac{\text{no. of equivalents of solute}}{\text{volume of solution (in L)}} $
$$ =n-factor\times molarity No.of equivalents=normality\times volume(in L) $$
F6 Equivalent mass =  $ \frac{Molar mass}{n-factor} $
F7 No. of equivalent =  $ \frac{Mass of the species}{equivalent mass} $  =  $ \frac{Mass of the species}{Molar mass} $
n - factor

Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: Solution/Answer for Q6
Content: (A) 56 mL
(B) 112 mL
(C) 168 mL
(D) 224 mL
Sol. 1000 mL  $ H_{2}O_{2} $   $ \longrightarrow $  liberates 20 L  $ O_{2} $  at STP
∴ 1 mL  $ H_{2}O_{2} $   $ \longrightarrow $   $ \frac{20}{1000} $  x 1000 mL  $ O_{2} $
5.6  $ \longrightarrow $  20  $ \times $  5.6 mL of  $ O_{2} $  = 112 mL of  $ O_{2} $
हल. 1000 mL  $ \longrightarrow $  H $ _{2} $ O $ _{2} $  मुक्त 20 L O $ _{2} $  (STP पर)
$$ 1mL H_{2}O_{2}\longrightarrow\frac{20}{1000}\times1000mL O_{2} $$
5.6→O_{2} 节 20 × 5.6 mL = O_{2} 节 112 mL

chunks.summary: This chapter provides a comprehensive overview of the Equivalent Concept in Chemistry, covering definitions of equivalent weight, n-factor (valency factor) calculation for elements, acids, bases, and salts, and the concept of Normality. It extensively details various types of titrations, including acid-base, redox (Permanganate), and Iodometric/Iodimetric methods, emphasizing the Law of Equivalence. The chapter also addresses advanced topics like the drawbacks of the equivalent concept, volume strength of hydrogen peroxide, water hardness measurement, and oleum strength calculation, supported by numerous solved examples.

Content: 9. A sample of hydrazine sulphate  $ [N_{2}H_{6}SO_{4}] $  was dissolved in 100 mL water. 10 mL of this solution was treated with excess of  $ FeCl_{3} $  Sol. Ferrous ions formed were estimated and it required 20 mL of M/50  $ KMnO_{4} $  solution in acidic medium.
(a) Write the balanced redox reactions.
(b) Estimate the amount of hydrazine sulphate in one litre of Sol.
$$ \mathsf{Fe^{3+}+N_{2}H_{4}\longrightarrow N_{2}+Fe^{2+}+H^{+}} $$
$$  MnO_{4}^{-}+Fe^{2+}+H^{+}\longrightarrow Mn^{2+}+Fe^{3+}+H_{2}O $$
Sol. (a) Given  $ 4Fe^{3+} + N_{2}H_{4} \longrightarrow N_{2} + 4Fe^{2+} + 4H^{+} $
$ MnO_{4}^{-} + 5Fe^{2+} + 8H^{+} \longrightarrow Mn^{2+} + 5Fe^{3+} + 4H_{2}O $
(b) In 10 mL solution, eq. of  $ N_{2}H_{6}SO_{4} = Eq. $  of  $ Fe^{2+} = Eq. $  of  $ KMnO_{4} $
$$  = 20 \times \frac{1}{50} \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 2 \times 10^{-3}  $$
v.f. of  $ N_{2}H_{6}SO_{4}=4 $
so, weight of  $ N_{2}H_{6}SO_{4} $  in 1 L solution =  $ \frac{2\times10^{-3}\times1000}{4\times10} $  × 130 = 6.5 g.

Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: APSP Solution 12: Determining n-factor for MnSO4 conversion
Content: 12. Eq. mass =  $ \frac{Molecular weight}{Change in oxidation no. of Mn} = \frac{Mol. wt.}{4 - 2} = \frac{Mol. wt.}{2} $
(O.N. of Mn in  $ MnSO_{4}=+2 $ ; O.N. of Mn in  $ MnO_{2}=+4 $ ).

chunks.context: JEE Advanced Solution Q21: Molarity calculation after partial neutralization
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: JEE Advanced Solution Q21: Molarity calculation after partial neutralization
Content: 21. In presence of phenophthalein indicator valence factor of  $ Hph = 1 $
Equivalents of HCl = Equivalent of  $ Na_{2}CO_{3} $
$ N_{1}V_{1} = N_{2}V_{2} $
For HCl  $ N_{1} = M_{1} $  and  $ Na_{2}CO_{3} N_{2} = N_{2} M_{1} V_{1} = M_{2} V_{2} $
$ 0.1 \times 100 = N_{2} \times 25 $
$ M_{2} = 0.4 $
Reaction is  $ Na_{2}CO_{3} + HCl \longrightarrow NaHCO_{3} + NaCl $
in moles of  $ Na_{2}CO_{3} = MV $
$ = 0.4018 \times 25 \times 10^{-3} $
= moles of  $ NaHCO_{3} $
molarity of  $ HCO_{3}^{-} = \frac{Moles of NaHCO_{3}}{Volume} = \frac{0.4 \times 25 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000}{125} = 0.08 $

context: Solved Example 17: Calculating Water Hardness and Washing Soda Requirement

chunks.summary: This chapter provides a comprehensive overview of the Equivalent Concept in Chemistry, covering definitions of equivalent weight, n-factor (valency factor) calculation for elements, acids, bases, and salts, and the concept of Normality. It extensively details various types of titrations, including acid-base, redox (Permanganate), and Iodometric/Iodimetric methods, emphasizing the Law of Equivalence. The chapter also addresses advanced topics like the drawbacks of the equivalent concept, volume strength of hydrogen peroxide, water hardness measurement, and oleum strength calculation, supported by numerous solved examples.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.context: Solved Example 17: Calculating Water Hardness and Washing Soda Requirement
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: Solved Example 17: Calculating Water Hardness and Washing Soda Requirement
Content: ## Solved Examples
Ex-17 0.00012% MgSO_{4} and 0.000111% CaCl_{2} is present in water. What is the measured hardness of water and millimoles of washing soda required to purify water 1000 L water ?
Sol. Basis of calculation = 100 g hard water
$$  MgSO_{4}=0.00012g=\frac{0.00012}{120}mole $$
$$ CaCl_{2}=0.000111g=\frac{0.000111}{111}mole $$
∴ equivalent moles of CaCO_{3} = \left(\frac{0.00012}{120} + \frac{0.000111}{111}\right) mole
∴ mass of CaCO_{3} =  $ \left(\frac{0.00012}{120} + \frac{0.000111}{111}\right) \times 100 = 2 \times 10^{-4} $  g
Hardness (in terms of ppm of CaCO₃) =  $ \frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{100} \times 10^{6} = 2 $  ppm
$$  CaCl_{2}+Na_{2}CO_{3}\longrightarrow CaCO_{3}+2NaCl $$
$$  NaSO_{4}+Na_{2}CO_{3}\longrightarrow MgCO_{3}+Na_{2}SO_{4} $$
∴ Required  $ Na_{2}CO_{3} $  for 100g of water =  $ \left(\frac{0.00012}{120} + \frac{0.000111}{111}\right) $  mole
$ = 2 \times 10^{-6} $  mole
∴ Required  $ Na_{2}CO_{3} $  for 1000 litre water  $ =\frac{2\times10^{-6}}{100}\times10^{6}=\frac{2}{100} $  mole ( $ \because $  d=1g/mL)
=\frac{20}{1000} \text{ mole} = 20 \text{ m mole}

combined_text:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: Solved Example 17: Calculating Water Hardness and Washing Soda Requirement
Content: ## Solved Examples
Ex-17 0.00012% MgSO_{4} and 0.000111% CaCl_{2} is present in water. What is the measured hardness of water and millimoles of washing soda required to purify water 1000 L water ?
Sol. Basis of calculation = 100 g hard water
$$  MgSO_{4}=0.00012g=\frac{0.00012}{120}mole $$
$$ CaCl_{2}=0.000111g=\frac{0.000111}{111}mole $$
∴ equivalent moles of CaCO_{3} = \left(\frac{0.00012}{120} + \frac{0.000111}{111}\right) mole
∴ mass of CaCO_{3} =  $ \left(\frac{0.00012}{120} + \frac{0.000111}{111}\right) \times 100 = 2 \times 10^{-4} $  g
Hardness (in terms of ppm of CaCO₃) =  $ \frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{100} \times 10^{6} = 2 $  ppm
$$  CaCl_{2}+Na_{2}CO_{3}\longrightarrow CaCO_{3}+2NaCl $$
$$  NaSO_{4}+Na_{2}CO_{3}\longrightarrow MgCO_{3}+Na_{2}SO_{4} $$
∴ Required  $ Na_{2}CO_{3} $  for 100g of water =  $ \left(\frac{0.00012}{120} + \frac{0.000111}{111}\right) $  mole
$ = 2 \times 10^{-6} $  mole
∴ Required  $ Na_{2}CO_{3} $  for 1000 litre water  $ =\frac{2\times10^{-6}}{100}\times10^{6}=\frac{2}{100} $  mole ( $ \because $  d=1g/mL)
=\frac{20}{1000} \text{ mole} = 20 \text{ m mole}

chunks.summary: This chapter covers the fundamental concepts of chemical solutions, including their types, methods for expressing concentration (mass %, mole fraction, molarity, molality), gas solubility governed by Henry's Law, vapor pressure lowering and Raoult's Law, colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure), and the concept of abnormal molar masses using the van't Hoff factor.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Henry's Law Statement and Formula
Content: Henry was the first to give a quantitative relation between pressure and solubility of a gas in a solvent which is known as Henry's law. The law states that at a constant temperature, the solubility of a gas in a liquid is directly proportional to the partial pressure of the gas present above the surface of liquid or solution. Dalton, a contemporary of Henry, also concluded independently that the solubility of a gas in a liquid solution is a function of partial
pressure of the gas. If we use the mole fraction of a gas in the solution as a measure of its solubility, then it can be said that the mole fraction of gas in the solution is proportional to the partial pressure of the gas over the solution. The most commonly used form of Henry's law states that "the partial pressure of the gas in vapour phase (p) is proportional to the mole fraction of the gas (x) in the solution" and is expressed as:
[Image Reference: Alt Text: "Image". Image Path: "images/CLASS-12/CHEMISTRY/Unit 1: Solutions/NCERT_Textbooks/img_in_chart_box_79_900_448_1263.jpg"]
### Fig. 1.2: Experimental results for the solubility of HCl gas in cyclohexane at 293 K. The slope of the line is the Henry's Law constant,  $ K_{H} $ .
$$ p=K_{\mathrm{H}}~x $$
Here  $ K_{H} $  is the Henry's law constant. If we draw a graph between partial pressure of the gas versus mole fraction of the gas in solution, then we should get a plot of the type as shown in Fig. 1.2.
Different gases have different  $ K_{H} $  values at the same temperature (Table 1.2). This suggests that  $ K_{H} $  is a function of the nature of the gas.

combined_text:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Henry's Law Statement and Formula
Content: Henry was the first to give a quantitative relation between pressure and solubility of a gas in a solvent which is known as Henry's law. The law states that at a constant temperature, the solubility of a gas in a liquid is directly proportional to the partial pressure of the gas present above the surface of liquid or solution. Dalton, a contemporary of Henry, also concluded independently that the solubility of a gas in a liquid solution is a function of partial
pressure of the gas. If we use the mole fraction of a gas in the solution as a measure of its solubility, then it can be said that the mole fraction of gas in the solution is proportional to the partial pressure of the gas over the solution. The most commonly used form of Henry's law states that "the partial pressure of the gas in vapour phase (p) is proportional to the mole fraction of the gas (x) in the solution" and is expressed as:
[Image Reference: Alt Text: "Image". Image Path: "images/CLASS-12/CHEMISTRY/Unit 1: Solutions/NCERT_Textbooks/img_in_chart_box_79_900_448_1263.jpg"]
### Fig. 1.2: Experimental results for the solubility of HCl gas in cyclohexane at 293 K. The slope of the line is the Henry's Law constant,  $ K_{H} $ .
$$ p=K_{\mathrm{H}}~x $$
Here  $ K_{H} $  is the Henry's law constant. If we draw a graph between partial pressure of the gas versus mole fraction of the gas in solution, then we should get a plot of the type as shown in Fig. 1.2.
Different gases have different  $ K_{H} $  values at the same temperature (Table 1.2). This suggests that  $ K_{H} $  is a function of the nature of the gas.

chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Vapour Pressure of Solids (Sublimation)
Content: ## (7) Saturated vapour pressure and solids: Sublimation
Solids can also loose particles from their surface to form a vapour, except that in this case we call the effect sublimation rather than evaporation. Sublimation is the direct change from solid to vapour without going through the liquid stage.
The forces of attraction in many solids are too high to allow much loss of particles from the surface. However, there are some solids which easily form vapours.
Naphthalene (used in "moth balls") has quite a strong smell. Molecules must be breaking away from the surface as a vapour, because otherwise we wouldn't be able to smell it.
Solid carbon dioxide never forms a liquid at atmospheric pressure and always converts directly from solid to vapour. That's why it is known as dry ice.

Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Partial Pressure and Saturation
Content: (4) Partial pressure of vapours: If vapours of a liquid are present in a gaseous mixture then,
Partial pressure of vapours of the liquid =  $ \frac{Pressure of vapours}{Total pressure} $
Saturation: A gas or gaseous mixture is said to be saturated with the vapours of a liquid if the partial pressure of the liquid vapours is equal to its (saturated) vapour pressure.

context: Example 1.6: Molar Mass Determination using Vapor Pressure Lowering

chunks.summary: This chapter covers the fundamental concepts of chemical solutions, including their types, methods for expressing concentration (mass %, mole fraction, molarity, molality), gas solubility governed by Henry's Law, vapor pressure lowering and Raoult's Law, colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure), and the concept of abnormal molar masses using the van't Hoff factor.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.context: Example 1.6: Molar Mass Determination using Vapor Pressure Lowering
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Example 1.6: Molar Mass Determination using Vapor Pressure Lowering
Content: ### Example 1.6
The vapour pressure of pure benzene at a certain temperature is 0.850 bar. A non-volatile, non-electrolyte solid weighing 0.5 g when added to 39.0 g of benzene (molar mass 78 g mol $ ^{-1} $ ). Vapour pressure of the solution, then, is 0.845 bar. What is the molar mass of the solid substance?
Solution The various quantities known to us are as follows:
$$ {p_{_{1}}}^{0}=0.850bar;p=0.845bar;M_{_{1}}=78g mol^{-1};w_{_{2}}=0.5g;w_{_{1}}=39g $$
Substituting these values in equation  $ (2.28) $ , we get
$$ \frac{0.850bar-0.845bar}{0.850bar}=\frac{0.5g\times78g mol^{-1}}{M_{2}\times39g} $$
Therefore,  $ M_{2} = 170 \, g mol^{-1} $

Content: # PART - IV : PRACTICE TEST-2 (IIT-JEE (ADVANCED Pattern))
Max. Time : 1 Hr.
Max. Marks : 66
## I mportant Instructions
A. General :
1. The test is of 1 hour duration.
2. The Test Booklet consists of 22 questions. The maximum marks are 66.
4. Section-1 contains 7 multiple choice questions. Each question has four choices (A), (B), (C) and (D) out of which ONE is correct.
5. Section-2 contains 5 multiple choice questions. Each question has four choices (A), (B), (C) and (D) out of which ONE OR MORE THAN ONE are correct.
6. Section 3 contains 6 questions. The answer to each of the questions is numerical value, ranging from 0 to 9 (both inclusive).
7. Section 4 contains 1 paragraphs each describing theory, experiment and data etc. 3 questions relate to paragraph. Each question pertaining to a particular passage should have only one correct answer among the four given choices (A), (B), (C) and (D).
8. Section 5 contains 1 multiple choice questions. Question has two lists (list-1: P, Q, R and S; List-2: 1, 2, 3 and 4). The options for the correct match are provided as (A), (B), (C) and (D) out of which ONLY ONE is correct.
9. For each question in Section 1, 4 and 5 you will be awarded 3 marks if you darken the bubble corresponding to the correct answer and zero mark if no bubble is darkened. In all other cases, minus one (-1) mark will be awarded.
10. For each question in Section 2, you will be awarded 3 marks. If you darken all the bubble(s) corresponding to the correct answer(s) and zero mark. If no bubbles are darkened. No negative marks will be answered for incorrect answer in this section.
11. For each question in Section 3, you will be awarded 3 marks if you darken only the bubble corresponding to the correct answer and zero mark if no bubble is darkened. No negative marks will be awarded for incorrect answers in this section.

Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: APSP Solution 16: Mixing acid solutions
Content: 16. Normality of a mixture (N) =  $ \frac{N_{1}V_{1} + N_{2}V_{2}}{V_{1} + V_{2}} $
$$  Normality(N_{1})of H_{2}SO_{4}=molarity\times basicity=0.2\times2=0.4N $$
$$ \mathsf{N}_{2}=0.2\times1=0.2\mathsf{N} $$
$$  V_{1}=100mL,V_{2}=200mL $$
$$ \mathsf{N}=\frac{\mathsf{N}_{1}\mathsf{V}_{1}+\mathsf{N}_{2}\mathsf{V}_{2}}{\mathsf{V}_{1}+\mathsf{V}_{2}}=\frac{40+40}{300}=\frac{80}{300}=0.2670\mathsf{N} $$
Normality of mixture of acid and base(N')
$$ \left(\mathsf{N}^{\prime}\right)=\frac{\mathsf{N}_{1}\mathsf{V}_{1}\sim\mathsf{N}_{2}\mathsf{V}_{2}}{\mathsf{V}_{1}+\mathsf{V}_{2}} $$

chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Effect of Temperature on Gas Solubility
Content: 6.4 Effect of temperature : Solubility of gases in liquids decreases with rise in temperature.
Explanation: When dissolved, the gas molecules are present in liquid phase and the process of dissolution can be considered similar to condensation and heat is evolved in this process. We have learnt that dissolution process involves dynamic equilibrium and thus must follow Le Chatelier's principle. As dissolution of gases in liquids is an exothermic process, the solubility should decrease with increase of temperature.
Note :  $ K_{H} $  values for both  $ N_{2} $  and  $ O_{2} $  increase with increase of temperature indicating that the solubility of gases increases with decrease of temperature. It is due to this reason that aquatic species are more comfortable in cold water rather than warm water.

chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Solved Examples: FPD and Colligative Comparison
Content: ## Solved Examples
Example-18 : Van't Hoff factors of aqueous solutions of X, Y, Z are 1.8, 0.8 and 2.5. Hence, their (assume equal concentrations in all three cases)
(A) b.p.: X < Y < Z
(C) osmotic pressure : X = Y = Z
(B) f. p. Z < X < Y
(D) v. p.: Y < X < Z
Solution : As van't Hoff factor increases RLVP increases i.e., V.P. decrease
Elevation in b.p. increases i.e., b.p. increases y < x < z
Depression in f.p. increases i.e., f.p. decreases y > x > z
Osmotic pressure increases so y < x < z. Ans. (B)
i.e., V.P. decreases y > x > z
Example-19 : 1000 g H₂O have 0.1 mole urea and its freezing point is -0.2°C and now it is freezed upto -2°C then how much amount of ice will form.
[Image Reference: Alt Text: "Image". Image Path: "images/CLASS-12/CHEMISTRY/Unit 1: Solutions/Resonance/Solution and Colligative Properties/img_in_image_box_349_1008_586_1191.jpg"]
Solution : It is assumed that solute do not freeze and do not vapourise
$$ \Delta\mathsf{T}_{\mathsf{F}}=0.2=\mathsf{K}_{\mathsf{f}}\frac{0.1}{1000}\times1000\quad\cdots\cdots(i) $$
$$ \Delta\mathsf{T}_{\mathsf{F}}=2=\mathsf{K}_{\mathsf{f}}\ \frac{0.1}{wt.\quad of\quad solvent}\ \times1000\ \ldots\ldots(ii)\ on dividing=\frac{wt.\quad of\quad solvent}{1000}\ .\ \frac{0.2}{2} $$
Weight of remaining  $ H_{2}O $  is 100 g and weight of ice is 900 g.

context: Comparison of Concentration Units and Example 1.3: Molality Calculation

chunks.summary: This chapter covers the fundamental concepts of chemical solutions, including their types, methods for expressing concentration (mass %, mole fraction, molarity, molality), gas solubility governed by Henry's Law, vapor pressure lowering and Raoult's Law, colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure), and the concept of abnormal molar masses using the van't Hoff factor.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.context: Comparison of Concentration Units and Example 1.3: Molality Calculation
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Comparison of Concentration Units and Example 1.3: Molality Calculation
Content: Each method of expressing concentration of the solutions has its own merits and demerits. Mass %, ppm, mole fraction and molality are independent of temperature, whereas molarity is a function of temperature. This is because volume depends on temperature and the mass does not.
Calculate molality of 2.5 g of ethanoic acid  $ \left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOH}\right) $  in 75 g of benzene. Example 1.3
Molar mass of  $ C_{2}H_{4}O_{2} $ :  $ 12 \times 2 + 1 \times 4 + 16 \times 2 = 60 \, g \, mol^{-1} $
Solution
Moles of  $ C_{2}H_{4}O_{2}=\frac{2.5\ g}{60\ g\ mol^{-1}}=0.0417\ mol $
Mass of benzene in kg = 75 g/1000 g kg $ ^{-1} $  = 75 × 10 $ ^{-3} $  kg
$$ \begin{aligned}Molality of C_{2}H_{4}O_{2}=\frac{Moles of C_{2}H_{4}O_{2}}{kg of benzene}=\frac{0.0417mol\times1000g kg^{-1}}{75g}\\=0.556mol kg^{-1}\end{aligned} $$

Mass of benzene in kg = 75 g/1000 g kg $ ^{-1} $  = 75 × 10 $ ^{-3} $  kg
$$ \begin{aligned}Molality of C_{2}H_{4}O_{2}=\frac{Moles of C_{2}H_{4}O_{2}}{kg of benzene}=\frac{0.0417mol\times1000g kg^{-1}}{75g}\\=0.556mol kg^{-1}\end{aligned} $$

chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Solved Example: Determining Coordination Number from OP
Content: Example-36 : (Note: Attempt this problem after you have studied co-ordination compounds)
Ba^{2+} ions, CN^{-} & Co^{2+} ions form a water soluble complex with Ba^{2+} ions as free cations. For a 0.01 M solution of this complex, osmotic pressure = 0.984 atm & degree of dissociation = 75%. Then find coordination number of Co^{2+} ion in this complex (T=300 K, R=0.082 L atm. mol^{-1} k^{-1})
| Resonance® Educating for better tomorrow | Reg. & Corp. Office : CG Tower, A-46 & 52, IPIA, Near City Mall, Jhalawar Road, Kota (Raj.) - 324005 |
| --- | --- |
| Website : www.resonance.ac.in | E-mail : contact@resonance.ac.in |
| Toll Free : 1800 258 5555 | CIN: U80302RJ2007PLC024029 |
| Solution & Colligative Properties |
| --- |
| Solution : | Say C.N. = x |
|   | 0.984 = i CRT |
|   | 0.984 = i 0.01 × 0.082 × 300 = i × 0.246 |
|   | i = 4 = 1 + (n - 1) α |
| ⇒ | n = 5 |
| Charge on co-ordination sphere = charge on Cobalt ion - charge on x cyanide ions = -(x - 2) i.e. co-ordination sphere is [Co(CN)x]-(x - 2) |
| Charge on Barium ion is +2 |
| Thus, formula of the complex will be Ba(x - 2) [Co(CN)x]2 by charge balance. |
| x - 2 + 2 = 5 |
| x = 5 |
| ∴ | CN = 5 |
| ∴ | Formula is Ba3[Co(CN)5)2. |
[Image Reference: Alt Text: "Image". Image Path: "images/CLASS-12/CHEMISTRY/Unit 1: Solutions/Resonance/Solution and Colligative Properties/img_in_image_box_101_210_1050_1376.jpg"]

chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Case II: Electrolyte Association (i vs beta)
Content: Der.4 Case - II : Electrolyte associates
Relation between degree of association β & i.
$$
\begin{array}{c|c|c|c}
nA & \longrightarrow & A_n. \\
t = 0 & C & 0 \\
t_{eq} & C(1-\beta) & \frac{C\beta}{n} \\
\end{array}
$$
Net concentration = C - C\beta +  $ \frac{C\beta}{n} $
$$
= C[1 + \left(\frac{1}{n} - 1\right)\beta]
$$
$$
i = 1 + \left(\frac{1}{n} - 1\right)\beta
$$
if dimerise n = 2 ; trimerise n = 3 ; tetramerise n = 4.
e.g. CH₃COOH 100% dimerise in benzene, i =  $ \frac{1}{2} $ ; C₆H₅COOH 100% dimerise in benzene, i =  $ \frac{1}{2} $

Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Solved Examples: Osmotic Pressure Calculations
Content: ## Solved Examples
Example-25 : Calculate osmotic pressure of 0.1 M urea aqueous solution at 300 K,
Solution : R = 0.082 lit atm K $ ^{-1} $
$$ \pi=CRT $$
$$ \pi=0.1\times0.082\times300 $$
$ \pi = 2.46 $  atm.
Example-26: If 10 g of an unknown substance (non-electrolytic) is dissolved to make 500 mL of solution, then osmotic pressure at 300 K is observed to be 1.23 atm find molecular weight?
| Resonance Educating for better tomorrow | Reg. & Corp. Office : CG Tower, A-46 & 52, IPIA, Near City Mall, Jhalawar Road, Kota (Raj.) - 324005 |
| --- | --- |
| Website : www.resonance.ac.in | E-mail : contact@resonance.ac.in |
| Toll Free : 1800 258 5555 | CIN: U80302RJ2007PLC024029 |
Solution :
Order of  $ \pi $  C > B > A.
Order of R.L.V.P C > B > A.
Order of V.P A > B > C.
Order of  $ \Delta T_{B} $  C > B > A.
Order of  $ T_{B} $  of solution C > B > A.
Order of  $ \Delta T_{F} $  C > B > A.
Order of  $ T_{F} $  of solution A > B > C.
Solution :  $ 1.23 = \frac{10 \times 1000}{M \times 500} \times 0.082 \times 300 $
$$  M = \frac{20}{1.23} \times \frac{0.082}{100} \times 300 \approx 400 \, \text{g/mol}  $$
Example-27: If 6 g of urea, 18 g glucose & 34.2 g sucrose is dissolved to make 500 mL of a solution at 300 K calculate osmotic pressure ?
Solution : molecular weight of urea = 60 g, Glucose = 180 g, Sucrose = 342 g
$ \pi = C \times 0.082 \times 300 $
$ \pi = \frac{0.3 \times 1000 \times 0.082 \times 300}{500} \Rightarrow 14.76 $  atm
Example-28: If 200 mL of 0.1 M urea solution is mixed with 300 mL of 0.2 M glucose solution at 300 K.
Calculate osmotic pressure?
0.02 moles urea
Solution :
0.06 moles glucose  $ \Rightarrow \pi = \frac{0.08}{0.5} \times 0.082 \times 300 = 3.94 $  atm.
Example-29 : If urea (aq) solution at 500K has O.P. = 2.05 atm. & glucose solution at 300 K has OP = 1.23 atm. If 200 ml of I $ ^{st} $  solution & 400 ml of 2 $ ^{nd} $  solution are mixed at 400 K then calculate O.P. of resulting solution at 400 K (assume molarity is not dependent on temp.)
Solution :
$$  C_{urea} = \frac{2.05}{R \times 500} = 0.05 \Rightarrow V_{urea} = 200 \, mL  $$
$$  C_{glucose} = \frac{1.23}{R \times 300} = 0.05 \Rightarrow V_{glucose} = 400 \, mL  $$
$$  C_{total} = \frac{C_{1} V_{1} + C_{2} V_{2}}{V_{1} + V_{2}} = \frac{0.05 \times 200 + 0.05 \times 400}{600} = 0.05  $$
$$  \pi = CRT = 0.05 \times 0.082 \times 400  $$
$$  \pi = 1.64 \, atm  $$

chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Dissolution and Crystallization Equilibrium
Content: Dissolution : When a solid solute is added to the solvent, some solute dissolves and its concentration increases in solution. This process is known as dissolution.
Crystallisation : Some solute particles in solution collide with the solid solute particles and get separated out of solution. This process is known as crystallisation.
At equilibrium, rate of dissolution is equal to rate of crystallisation. At this stage the concentration of solute in solution will remain constant under the given conditions, i.e., temperature and pressure. Such a solution is said to be saturated with the given solute.
$$  Solute+Solvent\rightleftharpoons Solution $$

Crystallisation : Some solute particles in solution collide with the solid solute particles and get separated out of solution. This process is known as crystallisation.
At equilibrium, rate of dissolution is equal to rate of crystallisation. At this stage the concentration of solute in solution will remain constant under the given conditions, i.e., temperature and pressure. Such a solution is said to be saturated with the given solute.
$$  Solute+Solvent\rightleftharpoons Solution $$

Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Solved Example: RLVP with Dissociation and Density
Content: Example-15 : If 0.1 M solutions of  $ K_{4} $  [Fe (CN) $ _{6} $ ] is prepared at 300 K then its density = 1.2 g/mL. If solute is 50% dissociated calculate  $ \Delta P $  of solution if P of pure water = 25 mm of Hg. (K = 39, Fe = 56)
Solution :
$$ i=1+(5-1)\times\frac{1}{2}=3. $$
$$ m=\frac{0.1\times1000}{1000\times1.2-1.2\times368} $$
$$ \frac{\mathsf{P}^{0}-\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{s}}}{\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{s}}}=\frac{\mathsf{i m}\times\mathsf{M}}{1000}=\left(\frac{3\times0.1\times1000}{1000\times1.2-1.2\times368}\right)\times\frac{18}{1000} $$
$$ \frac{\mathsf{P}^{0}}{\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{s}}}=1+7.12\times10^{-3} $$
$$  P_{s}=24.82\ mm\ of\ Hg $$
$$ \Delta p=25-24.82=0.18mm of Hg $$

$$ i=1+(5-1)\times\frac{1}{2}=3. $$

chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Minimum Boiling Azeotropes
Content: (1) D27 Minimum Boiling Azeotropes : Non-ideal solutions showing large positive deviation from Raoult's law form minimum boiling azeotropes which boil at temperature lower than boiling point of its components 'A' and 'B', e.g., water and benzene, chloroform and methanol.
The figures below show the Temperature vs. composition (at constant pressure) phase diagram on the left side and Pressure vs. composition (at constant temperature) phase diagram for a minimum-boiling azeotropic mixture chloroform and methanol.
Note: The detailed values in the diagram are not important. Only the qualitative shape of the diagram is important.
Temperature-Minimum Azeotrope Mixture of Chloroform and Methanol
[Image Reference: Alt Text: "Image". Image Path: "images/CLASS-12/CHEMISTRY/Unit 1: Solutions/Resonance/Solution and Colligative Properties/img_in_chart_box_179_337_592_780.jpg"]
At the lowest point in the temperature vs. composition phase diagram, the concentration in the vapour phase is the same as the concentration in the liquid phase  $ (y = x) $ . This concentration is known as the azeotropic composition. At this point, the mixture boils at a constant temperature and without change in composition.

The figures below show the Temperature vs. composition (at constant pressure) phase diagram on the left side and Pressure vs. composition (at constant temperature) phase diagram for a minimum-boiling azeotropic mixture chloroform and methanol.
Note: The detailed values in the diagram are not important. Only the qualitative shape of the diagram is important.

At the lowest point in the temperature vs. composition phase diagram, the concentration in the vapour phase is the same as the concentration in the liquid phase  $ (y = x) $ . This concentration is known as the azeotropic composition. At this point, the mixture boils at a constant temperature and without change in composition.

chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: Comprehension 1: Definition of Equivalent Mass
Content: Equivalent Mass :
The equivalent mass of a substance is defined as the number of parts by mass of it which combine with or displace 1.0078 parts by mass of hydrogen, 8 parts by mass of oxygen and 35.5 parts by mass of chlorine.
The equivalent mass of a substance expressed in grams is called gram equivalent mass.
The equivalent mass of a substance is not constant. It depends upon the reaction in which the substance is participating. A compound may have different equivalent mass in different chemical reactions and under different experimental conditions.
(A) Equivalent mass of an acid : It is the mass of an acid in grams which contains 1.0078 g of replaceable  $ H^{+} $  ions or it is the mass of acid which contains one mole of replaceable  $ H^{+} $  ions. It may be calculated as :
Basicity of acid = number of replaceable hydrogen atoms present in one molecule of acid
(B) Equivalent mass of a base : It is the mass of the base which contains one mole of replaceable OH $ ^{-} $  ions in molecule.
Acidity of base = Number of replaceable  $ OH^{-} $  ions present in one molecule of the base
Equivalent mass of an oxidising agent :
## अनुच्छेद #1
## तुल्योंक द्रव्यमान :
तुल्यांक द्रव्यमान पदार्थ का वह द्रव्यमान है, जो हाइड्रोजन के द्रव्यमान से 1.0078 भाग, ऑक्सीजन के द्रव्यमान से 8 भाग तथा क्लोरीन के द्रव्यमान से 35.5 भाग के साथ संयोजित होता है। एक पदार्थ के तुल्यांक द्रव्यमान को ग्राम में व्यक्त करने पर ग्राम तुल्यांक द्रव्यमान कहा जाता है।
एक पदार्थ का तुल्यांक द्रव्यमान नियत नहीं होता है। यह उस अभिक्रिया पर निर्भर करता है, जिसमें पदार्थ भाग लेता है। एक यौगिक का भिन्न रासायनिक अभिक्रियाओं तथा भिन्न प्रायोगिक परिस्थितियों में भिन्न तुल्यांक द्रव्यमान होता है।
(A) एक अम्ल का तुल्यांक द्रव्यमान : यह एक अम्ल का ग्राम में वह द्रव्यमान है, जिसमें विस्थापित H⁺ आयन के 1.0078 ग्राम होते हैं अथवा यह अम्ल का वह द्रव्यमान है जिसमें विस्थापित H⁺ आयन के एक मोल होते हैं। इसे निम्न प्रकार से परिकलित किया जाता है :
अम्ल की धारकता = अम्ल के एक अणु में उपस्थित विस्थापित हाइड्रोजन परमाणुओं की संख्या
(B) एक धार का तुल्योंक द्रव्यमान : यह धार का वह द्रव्यमान है जो अणु में विस्थापित OH⁻ के एक मोल रखता है।
क्षार की अम्लता = क्षार के एक अणु में उपस्थित विस्थापित OH- आयनों की संख्या एक ऑक्सीकारक का तुल्यांक द्रव्यमान
(a) इलेक्ट्रॉन संकल्पना : ऑक्सीकारक का तुल्यांक द्रव्यमान =  $ \frac{\text{ऑक्सीकारक का आणिवक द्रव्यमान}}{\text{एक अणु द्वारा प्राप्त इलेक्ट्रॉन की संख्या}} $
(b) ऑक्सीकरण संख्या संकल्पना : ऑक्सीकारक का तुल्यांक द्रव्यमान =
ऑक्सीकारक के लिए प्रति अणु ऑक्सीकरण संख्या। में कुल परिवर्तन
अनुच्छेद #3
##### नीचे दी गयी टेबल के तीन कॉलमों में उपलब्ध सूचना का उपयुक्त ढंग से सुमेल कर प्रश्नों Q.7, Q.8 और Q.9 के उत्तर दीजिये।
$$ \mathsf{E}=\frac{\mathsf{M}\omega}{\mathsf{n}-\mathsf{f a c t o r}} $$

chunks.context: Solved Example: Benzene/Toluene Ideal Solution Phase Diagram Analysis
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Solved Example: Benzene/Toluene Ideal Solution Phase Diagram Analysis
Content: ## Solved Examples
Example-9 : Benzene and toluene form nearly ideal solutions. At 300 K,  $ P^{o}_{toluene} = 30 $  mm Hg and  $ P^{o}_{benzene} = 100 $  mm Hg.
A liquid mixture is composed of 3 mol of toluene and 2 mol of benzene.
(a) If the pressure over the mixture at 300 K is reduced. At what pressure does the first vapour form?
(b) What is the composition of the first trace of vapour formed?
(c) If the pressure is reduced further, at what pressure does the last trace of liquid disappear?
(d) What is the composition of the last trace of liquid?
## Ans
(a) 58 mm Hg (b)  $ y_{b}=20/29 $  (c) 250/6 mm Hg (d)  $ x_{b}=1/6 $
## Solution :
(a)  $ P = X_{A}P^{0}_{A} + X_{B}P^{0}_{B} = \frac{3}{5} \times 30 + \frac{2}{5} \times 100 = 58 \, mm $  Hg
(b) Composition of the first trace of vapour formed
$$ \mathsf{Y}_{\mathsf{benzene}}=\frac{\mathsf{X}_{\mathsf{b}}\mathsf{P}^{0}_{\mathsf{b}}}{\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{Total}}}=\frac{2}{5}\times\frac{100}{58}=\frac{20}{29} $$
&
$$ \mathsf{Y}_{\mathsf{toluene}}=1-\frac{20}{29}=\frac{9}{29} $$
(c) The last trace of liquid disappear at  $ Y_{A} = 3/5 $
$ Y_{B} = 2/5 $
$$ \frac{1}{\mathsf{p}}=\frac{\mathsf{Y}_{\mathsf{A}}}{\mathsf{P}^{0}_{\mathsf{A}}}+\frac{\mathsf{Y}_{\mathsf{B}}}{\mathsf{P}^{0}_{\mathsf{B}}} $$
$$ \frac{1}{p}=\frac{3}{5\times30}+\frac{2}{5\times100} $$
or
$$ p=\frac{250}{6}\ mm\ Hg $$
(d) The composition of the last trace of liquid
$$ \mathsf{X}^{\prime}_{benzene}=\frac{\mathsf{Y}_{benzene}\mathsf{P}_{Total}}{\mathsf{P}_{b}^{\ 0}}=\frac{\frac{2}{5}\times\frac{250}{6}}{100}=\frac{1}{6} $$
SO
$$ X^{\prime}toluene=\frac{5}{6}. $$

combined_text:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Solved Example: Benzene/Toluene Ideal Solution Phase Diagram Analysis
Content: ## Solved Examples
Example-9 : Benzene and toluene form nearly ideal solutions. At 300 K,  $ P^{o}_{toluene} = 30 $  mm Hg and  $ P^{o}_{benzene} = 100 $  mm Hg.
A liquid mixture is composed of 3 mol of toluene and 2 mol of benzene.
(a) If the pressure over the mixture at 300 K is reduced. At what pressure does the first vapour form?
(b) What is the composition of the first trace of vapour formed?
(c) If the pressure is reduced further, at what pressure does the last trace of liquid disappear?
(d) What is the composition of the last trace of liquid?
## Ans
(a) 58 mm Hg (b)  $ y_{b}=20/29 $  (c) 250/6 mm Hg (d)  $ x_{b}=1/6 $
## Solution :
(a)  $ P = X_{A}P^{0}_{A} + X_{B}P^{0}_{B} = \frac{3}{5} \times 30 + \frac{2}{5} \times 100 = 58 \, mm $  Hg
(b) Composition of the first trace of vapour formed
$$ \mathsf{Y}_{\mathsf{benzene}}=\frac{\mathsf{X}_{\mathsf{b}}\mathsf{P}^{0}_{\mathsf{b}}}{\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{Total}}}=\frac{2}{5}\times\frac{100}{58}=\frac{20}{29} $$
&
$$ \mathsf{Y}_{\mathsf{toluene}}=1-\frac{20}{29}=\frac{9}{29} $$
(c) The last trace of liquid disappear at  $ Y_{A} = 3/5 $
$ Y_{B} = 2/5 $
$$ \frac{1}{\mathsf{p}}=\frac{\mathsf{Y}_{\mathsf{A}}}{\mathsf{P}^{0}_{\mathsf{A}}}+\frac{\mathsf{Y}_{\mathsf{B}}}{\mathsf{P}^{0}_{\mathsf{B}}} $$
$$ \frac{1}{p}=\frac{3}{5\times30}+\frac{2}{5\times100} $$
or
$$ p=\frac{250}{6}\ mm\ Hg $$
(d) The composition of the last trace of liquid
$$ \mathsf{X}^{\prime}_{benzene}=\frac{\mathsf{Y}_{benzene}\mathsf{P}_{Total}}{\mathsf{P}_{b}^{\ 0}}=\frac{\frac{2}{5}\times\frac{250}{6}}{100}=\frac{1}{6} $$
SO
$$ X^{\prime}toluene=\frac{5}{6}. $$

chunks.summary: Comprehensive study guide covering solutions, concentration terms (mass %, Molarity, Molality, Normality, Mole Fraction, ppm), vapor pressure concepts (Raoult's Law, Henry's Law, Azeotropes), and colligative properties (RLVP, Boiling Point Elevation, Freezing Point Depression, Osmotic Pressure), including Vant'Hoff factor for non-ideal behavior.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: EBP Formula and Constants
Content: ## Elevation of Boiling point of any solution :
Since vapour pressure of solution is smaller than vapour pressure of pure solvent at any temperature hence to make it equal to  $ P_{ext} $ , we have to increase the temperature of solution by greater amount in comparison to pure solvent.
| Der.7 | ΔTb = Tb − Tb0 |
| --- | --- |
| ΔTb ∝ m |
| m = Molality |
| F27 | ΔTb = Kbm |
Note: (i) If solute gets associated/dissociated then $\Delta T_{b}=i\times K_{b}\times molality$
(ii) Units of  $ K_{b} $ :  $ \frac{\Delta T_{b}}{molality} = \frac{K}{mol/kg} $
Thus units of  $ K_{b} = K $  kg mol $ ^{-1} $
(iii)  $ K_{b} $  is dependent on property of solvent and known as ebullioscopic constant of solvent
D34 It is equal to elevation in boiling point of 1molal solution. It is also called molal elevation constant. The units of  $ K_{b} $ , is K/m or °C/m or K kg mol $ ^{-1} $ .
$$ K_{b}=\frac{RT_{b}^{2}\ M}{1000\times\Delta H_{vap}}=\frac{RT_{b}^{2}}{1000\times L_{vap}} $$
where, $\Delta H_{\mathrm{vap}}$ is molar enthalpy of vaporisation (cal/mol or J/mol)
$L_{\mathrm{Vap}}$ is Latent Heat of Vapourisation in cal/g or J/g
M is molar mass of the solvent in gram
$R = 2 \text{ cal mol}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$ or 8.314 J/mol-K
$T_{\mathrm{b}} = \text{Boiling point of pure liquid solvent (in kelvin)}$
$L_{\mathrm{vap}} = \left( \frac{\Delta H_{\mathrm{vap}}}{M} \right)$
(iv) Elevation in boiling point is proportional to the lowering of vapour pressure i.e. $\Delta T_{b} \propto \Delta P$
For water  $ L_{vap} = 540 $  Cal/g,  $ T_{b} = 100^{\circ}C $
$ \Rightarrow $   $ K_{b}=\frac{2\times373\times373}{1000\times540}=Kkgmol^{-1}=0.52Kkg $

chunks.summary: This chapter covers the fundamental concepts of chemical solutions, including their types, methods for expressing concentration (mass %, mole fraction, molarity, molality), gas solubility governed by Henry's Law, vapor pressure lowering and Raoult's Law, colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure), and the concept of abnormal molar masses using the van't Hoff factor.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Abnormal Molar Masses Introduction (Dissociation)
Content: ### 1.7 Abnormal Molar Masses
We know that ionic compounds when dissolved in water dissociate into cations and anions. For example, if we dissolve one mole of KCl (74.5 g) in water, we expect one mole each of  $ K^{+} $ and  $ Cl^{-} $ ions to be released in the solution. If this happens, there would be two moles of particles in the solution. If we ignore interionic attractions, one mole of KCl in one kg of water would be expected to increase the boiling point by  $ 2 \times 0.52 $  K = 1.04 K. Now if we did not know about the degree of
dissociation, we could be led to conclude that the mass of 2 mol particles is 74.5 g and the mass of one mole of KCl would be 37.25 g. This brings into light the rule that, when there is dissociation of solute into ions, the experimentally determined molar mass is always lower than the true value.

context: Depression of Freezing Point Definition and Concept

chunks.summary: This chapter covers the fundamental concepts of chemical solutions, including their types, methods for expressing concentration (mass %, mole fraction, molarity, molality), gas solubility governed by Henry's Law, vapor pressure lowering and Raoult's Law, colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure), and the concept of abnormal molar masses using the van't Hoff factor.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.context: Depression of Freezing Point Definition and Concept
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Depression of Freezing Point Definition and Concept
Content: #### 1.6.3 Depression of Freezing Point
The lowering of vapour pressure of a solution causes a lowering of the freezing point compared to that of the pure solvent (Fig. 1.8). We know that at the freezing point of a substance, the solid phase is in dynamic equilibrium with the liquid phase. Thus, the freezing point of a substance may be defined as the temperature at which the vapour pressure of the substance in its liquid phase is equal to its vapour pressure in the solid phase. A solution will freeze when its vapour pressure equals the vapour pressure of the pure solid solvent as is clear from Fig. 1.8. According to Raoult's law, when a non-volatile solid is added to the solvent its vapour pressure decreases and now it would become equal to that of solid solvent at lower temperature. Thus, the freezing point of the solvent decreases.
[Image Reference: Alt Text: "Image". Image Path: "images/CLASS-12/CHEMISTRY/Unit 1: Solutions/NCERT_Textbooks/img_in_image_box_118_686_538_1081.jpg"]
### Fig. 1.8: Diagram showing  $ \Delta T_{f} $ , depression of the freezing point of a solvent in a solution.
Let  $ T_{f}^{0} $  be the freezing point of pure solvent and  $ T_{f} $  be its freezing point when non-volatile solute is dissolved in it. The decrease in freezing point.
$ \Delta T_{f} = T_{f}^{0} - T_{f} $  is known as depression in freezing point.

Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Definition of Saturated Vapour Pressure (V.P.)
Content: In this equilibrium, there will be a fixed number of the gaseous particles in the space above the liquid. When these particles hit the walls of the container, they exert a pressure. This pressure is called the saturated vapour pressure (or just vapour pressure) of the liquid.
Suppose, we take 10 gram liquid in a chamber. Initially, there are no molecules in the gaseous phase. Thus, no pressure is exerted on the mercury and the level of mercury in both columns is equal. Suppose, after vapourization of 5 gram of the liquid vapour-liquid equilibrium is established. Now, molecules in the gaseous phase exert pressure on the mercury and level of mercury in the column attached to the chamber decreases.
Note: From the figure, we can see that, Vapour Pressure of the liquid = h mm Hg.
(Imagine no liquid molecule exist in vapour phase)
Note: Volatile solids (like Iodine) also evaporate and have a vapour pressure at any given temperature, just like liquids.

chunks.summary: This chapter provides a comprehensive overview of the Equivalent Concept in Chemistry, covering definitions of equivalent weight, n-factor (valency factor) calculation for elements, acids, bases, and salts, and the concept of Normality. It extensively details various types of titrations, including acid-base, redox (Permanganate), and Iodometric/Iodimetric methods, emphasizing the Law of Equivalence. The chapter also addresses advanced topics like the drawbacks of the equivalent concept, volume strength of hydrogen peroxide, water hardness measurement, and oleum strength calculation, supported by numerous solved examples.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.context: Solved Example 18: Calculating Water Volume for Oleum Dilution
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: Solved Example 18: Calculating Water Volume for Oleum Dilution
Content: ## Solved Examples
Ex-18 What volume of water is required (in mL) to prepare 1 L of 1 M solution of  $ H_{2}SO_{4} $  (density = 1.5g/mL) by using 109% oleum and water only (Take density of pure water = 1 g/mL).
Sol. 1 mole  $ H_{2}SO_{4} $  in 1L solution = 98 g  $ H_{2}SO_{4} $  in 1500 g solution = 98 g  $ H_{2}SO_{4} $  in 1402 g water. Also, in 109% oleum, 9 g  $ H_{2}O $  is required to form 109 g pure  $ H_{2}SO_{4} $  & so, to prepare 98 g  $ H_{2}SO_{4} $ , water needed is  $ 9/109 \times 98 = 8.09 $  g.
Total water needed = 1402 + 8.09 = 1410.09 g = 1410.09 mL

$$ 30\times\frac{1}{12}=20\times N^{\prime} $$

∴ Strength = N' × equivalent mass =  $ \frac{1}{8} $  × 17 = 2.12 g/L.

Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Derivation for Mixing Molar Solutions
Content: Der.1 If V₁ mL of C₁ molarity solution is mixed with V₂ mL of C₂ molarity solution (same substance or solute)
∴ C₁(V₁ + V₂) = C₁V₁ + C₂V₂
C₁ =  $ \left[\frac{C₁V₁ + C₂V₂}{V₁ + V₂}\right] = \frac{\text{Total moles}}{\text{Total volume}} $  where C₁ = molarity of final solution

chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Azeotropic Mixtures
Content: 7.5 Azeotropic Mixtures: Very large deviations from ideality lead to a special class of mixtures known as azeotropes, azeotropic mixtures, or constant-boiling mixtures.
D26 Azeotropes : Liquid mixtures which distill over without changes in composition are called constant boiling mixtures or Azeotropes or Azeotropic mixtures.
A boiling liquid mixture at the azeotropic composition produces a vapour of exactly the same composition, and the liquid does not change its composition as it evaporates. Two types of azeotropes are known.

A boiling liquid mixture at the azeotropic composition produces a vapour of exactly the same composition, and the liquid does not change its composition as it evaporates. Two types of azeotropes are known.

chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Miscellaneous Solved Problems (MSPs) - Concentration Conversions
Content: # MISCELLANEOUS SOLVED PROBLEMS (MSPs)
1. A 6.90 M solution of KOH in water has 30% by weight of KOH. Calculate density of solution.
Sol. Let V = 1 It, then moles of solute = 6.9
wt of solute = 6.9 × 56 gm
$$ \%=\frac{M_{solute}}{M_{solution}}\times100 $$
$$ \mathrm{S o,}=\frac{6.9\times56}{\mathrm{d}\times1000}\times100=30; $$
2. 10 ml of sulphuric acid solution (sp. gr. = 1.84) contains 98% by weight of pure acid. Calculate the volume of 2.5 M NaOH solution required to just neutralise the acid.
Sol. Wt of solute = 10 × 1.84 ×  $ \frac{98}{100} $  g
So moles of solute =  $ \frac{18.4}{98} \times \frac{98}{100} = 0.184 $
$$ \mathsf{n_{H^{+}}}=2\times0.184 $$
$ 2 \times 0.184 = \frac{2.5 \times V}{1000} $  V = 147.2
3. A sample of  $ H_{2}SO_{4} $  (density 1.8 g mL $ ^{-1} $ ) is labelled as 74.66% by weight. What is molarity of acid?
Sol. Let V = 1lt = 1000 ml
So mass of solution = 1800 gm
So mass of solute = 1800 ×  $ \frac{74.66}{100} $  g
So molarity = moles of solute in 1lt =  $ \frac{18 \times 74.66}{98} $  = 13.71 M
4. The density of 3M solution of  $ Na_{2}S_{2}O_{3} $  is 1.25 g mL $ ^{-1} $ . Calculate.
(a) the % by weight of  $ Na_{2}S_{2}O_{3} $
(b) mole fraction of  $ Na_{2}S_{2}O_{3} $
Ans.
(a) 37.92 (b) 0.065
Sol. (a)  $ m_{solution} = 1000 \times 1.25 = 1250 $  gm
Mass of  $ Na_{2}S_{2}O_{3}=3\times158=474 $
$$ \%\ (w/w)=\frac{474}{1250}\times100=37.92 $$
(b)  $ X_{Na_{2}S_{2}O_{3}} = \frac{3}{3 + 43.11} = 0.065. $
5. Calculate Molality of aqueous urea solution which has  $ X_{urea} = 0.2 $
Sol. Molality =  $ \frac{1000X_{1}}{(1-X_{1})m_{2}}=\frac{1000\times0.2}{0.8\times18}=13.88. $

Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: JEE Main Q3: Nitrogen Percentage via Kjeldahl's Method
Content: 3. 1.4 g of an organic compound was digested according to Kjeldahl's method and the ammonia evolved was absorbed in 60 mL of M/10 H₂SO₄ solution. The excess sulphuric acid required 20 mL of M/10 NaOH solution for neutralization. The percentage of nitrogen in the compound is :
[JEE(Main) 2015 Online (10-04-15), 4/120]
1.4 ग्राम कार्बनिक यौगिक को जेल्डाल विधि द्वारा अभिकृत किया गया तथा निकलने वाली अमोनिया गैस M/10 H₂SO₄ के 60 mL द्वारा अवशोपित हुई। सल्फ्युरिक अम्ल के आधिक्य को उदासीन करने के लिए M/10 NaOH के 20 mL की आवश्यकता है। यौगिक में नाइट्रोजन की प्रतिशतता बताइये।
[JEE(Main) 2015 Online (10-04-15), 4/120]
(1) 24
(2) 5
(3) 10
(4) 3

chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Reason for FPD
Content: ## Reason for Depression In Freezing Point :
At the freezing point, the vapour pressure of solid and liquid is equal. When non-volatile solute is dissolved in the solvent, the vapour pressure of solvent in the solution decreases. It means vapour pressure of solid and liquid solvent will become equal at lower temperature, i.e., freezing point of solvent in solution is lower than that of pure solvent.

It means vapour pressure of solid and liquid solvent will become equal at lower temperature, i.e., freezing point of solvent in solution is lower than that of pure solvent.

chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Equivalent Concept and Titration
Context: JEE Offline Solution Q5: Kjeldahl's calculation
Content: 5. Mass of organic compound = 1.4 g
let it contain x mmole of N atom.
organic compound → NH₃
x m mole
2NH₃ + H₂SO₄ → (NH₄)₂SO₄. (1st)
6 mmole
initially taken.
H₂SO₄ + 2NaOH → Na₂SO₄ + 2H₂O (2nd)
2 mmole
reacted
Hence m moles of H₂SO₄ reacted in 2nd equation = 1
⇒ m moles of H₂SO₄ reacted from 1st equation = 6 - 1 = 5 m moles
⇒ m moles of NH₃ in 1st equation = 2 × 5 = 10 m moles
⇒ m moles of N atom in the organic compound = 10 m moles
⇒ mass of N = 10 × 10⁻³ × 14 = 0.14 g
⇒ % of N =  $ \frac{0.14}{1.4} \times 100 = 10\% $

context: Non-ideal Solutions: Positive and Negative Deviations

chunks.summary: This chapter covers the fundamental concepts of chemical solutions, including their types, methods for expressing concentration (mass %, mole fraction, molarity, molality), gas solubility governed by Henry's Law, vapor pressure lowering and Raoult's Law, colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure), and the concept of abnormal molar masses using the van't Hoff factor.
chunks.subject: CHEMISTRY
chunks.context: Non-ideal Solutions: Positive and Negative Deviations
chunks.document:
Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: 
Context: Non-ideal Solutions: Positive and Negative Deviations
Content: When a solution does not obey Raoult's law over the entire range of concentration, then it is called non-ideal solution. The vapour pressure of such a solution is either higher or lower than that predicted by Raoult's law (equation 1.16). If it is higher, the solution exhibits positive deviation and if it is lower, it exhibits negative deviation from Raoult's law. The plots of vapour pressure as a function of mole fractions for such solutions are shown in Fig. 1.6.
The cause for these deviations lies in the nature of interactions at the molecular level. In case of positive deviation from Raoult's law, A-B interactions are weaker than those between A-A or B-B, i.e., in this case the intermolecular attractive forces between the solute-solvent molecules are weaker than those between the solute-solute and solvent-solvent molecules. This means that in such solutions, molecules of A (or B) will find it easier to escape than in pure state. This will increase the vapour
### Fig.1.6
The vapour pressures of two component systems as a function of composition (a) a solution that shows positive deviation from Raoult's law and (b) a solution that shows negative deviation from Raoult's law.
[Image Reference: Alt Text: "Image". Image Path: "images/CLASS-12/CHEMISTRY/Unit 1: Solutions/NCERT_Textbooks/img_in_chart_box_374_129_683_486.jpg"]
### (a)
[Image Reference: Alt Text: "Image". Image Path: "images/CLASS-12/CHEMISTRY/Unit 1: Solutions/NCERT_Textbooks/img_in_chart_box_737_123_1036_480.jpg"]
### (b)
pressure and result in positive deviation. Mixtures of ethanol and acetone behave in this manner. In pure ethanol, molecules are hydrogen bonded. On adding acetone, its molecules get in between the host molecules and break some of the hydrogen bonds between them. Due to weakening of interactions, the solution shows positive deviation from Raoult's law [Fig. 1.6 (a)]. In a solution formed by adding carbon disulphide to acetone, the dipolar interactions between solute-solvent molecules are weaker than the respective interactions among the solute-solute and solvent-solvent molecules. This solution also shows positive deviation.
In case of negative deviations from Raoult's law, the intermolecular attractive forces between A-A and B-B are weaker than those between A-B and leads to a decrease in vapour pressure resulting in negative deviations. An example of this type is a mixture of phenol and aniline. In this case the intermolecular hydrogen bonding between phenolic proton and lone pair on nitrogen atom of aniline is stronger than the respective intermolecular hydrogen bonding between similar molecules. Similarly, a mixture of chloroform and acetone forms a solution with negative deviation from Raoult's law. This is because chloroform molecule is able to form hydrogen bond with acetone molecule as shown.
[Image Reference: Alt Text: "Image". Image Path: "images/CLASS-12/CHEMISTRY/Unit 1: Solutions/NCERT_Textbooks/img_in_image_box_413_1039_674_1121.jpg"]
This decreases the escaping tendency of molecules for each component and consequently the vapour pressure decreases resulting in negative deviation from Raoult's law [Fig. 1.6. (b)].

chunks.summary: Comprehensive study guide covering solutions, concentration terms (mass %, Molarity, Molality, Normality, Mole Fraction, ppm), vapor pressure concepts (Raoult's Law, Henry's Law, Azeotropes), and colligative properties (RLVP, Boiling Point Elevation, Freezing Point Depression, Osmotic Pressure), including Vant'Hoff factor for non-ideal behavior.

Subject: CHEMISTRY
Topic: Solution and Colligative Properties
Context: Definition of Parts Per Million (ppm)
Content: 3.8 D18 Parts per million (ppm): The number of parts of solute present in 1 million parts of solution are called its ppm. When a solute is present in small quantities (very minute amounts), it is easier to express the concentration in parts per million.
